responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Abstracts of the Annual Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Initial Validity of a Computerized Text Analysis Measure of Reflective Function

Eric Fertuck , Erhard Mergenthaler , Mary Target and John Clarkin

John Clarkin

1051 Riverside Drive
New York, NY 10032
Fax: 212-543-6946
E-mail: ef304@columbia.edu

Germany

John Clarkin  

At its January meetings earlier this year, the American Psycho-analytic Association held the second of its annual peer-reviewed poster sessions highlighting the many research studies being conducted by analytic investigators here and abroad.Linda Goodman, Linda Mayes, and I, as a subcommittee of the association's Committee on Scientific Activities, continue to serve as organizers of the sessions. The nineteen poster abstracts presented here reflect a new emphasis in our field on systematic empirical studies addressing a rich spectrum of research topics conceptually relevant to psychoanalytic theory, current clinical practice, and outcomes. Many of the posters are reports of new and ongoing investigations intended to draw the reader into dialogue with the investigators. As the poster sessions are designed to generate interest and discussion around these endeavors, readers are encouraged to e-mail or otherwise contact the posters' authors.

—Stuart T. Hauser

Reflective Function (RF)

responseSet.title: Our Literature
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Our Literature

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

Our literature, a portion of which has recently come under my stewardship, plays many important roles in the ongoing life of every psychoanalyst. We become analysts in part by learning our literature. It is one way of teaching our profession. Our literature is our history, populated by a parade of heroes and villains.

We would be hard pressed to find another field in which this one's or that one's paper plays as crucial a role in setting in motion the neophyte's conceptual thinking and new identity formation. Our literature links us to Freud, to one another, and to what we will eventually understand and do. And it invariably does so in a manner that illuminates the huge divide between the elegant abstractness of our analytic words and the crude, confusing, even worrisome nature of our clinical work.

Our literature quickly becomes part of our ideal; beloved, feared, and despised. And the characters, the way we identify them with their papers—Hartmann's paper about this, Winnicott's paper about that. I am reminded here of a scene that took place at the Waldorf twenty or so years ago, then as now the temple of art deco splendor we consider the appropriate setting for our midwinter thoughts. During the discussion of a presentation long since forgotten, a contentious debate erupted on the merits and perils of the therapeutic alliance concept. The back-and-forth took the usual twists and turns, eventually settling on the conviction that the truth, or at least enlightenment, hinged on Sterba's paper on the fate of the ego in analytic therapy. What did Sterba mean? Was he referring to the metaphorical or the actual, the volitional or the unconscious, the temporary or the sustained? Temperatures rose, the battle widened, pedigrees were invoked. What Sterba meant in that classic paper was the key! From the back of the room, an elderly man approached the microphone and announced, “I am Richard Sterba and what I meant was the following.” A ghost had arisen: we were mesmerized. It could not be true—Sterba's paper, written by Richard Sterba, this man right here. No one seemed to listen to him.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Quantitative Studies of Psychoanalytic Transcripts Using Data that can be Processed with a Personal Computer and Safely Shared

George Klumpner and Thomas Klumpner

Thomas Klumpner

11 Elizabeth Court
Oak Park, IL 60302-2009

Thomas Klumpner  

We have devised methods to extract quantitative measures of data from transcripts of audio-recorded psychoanalytic treatments. Unlike transcripts,

1244

our quantitative data and measures can be safely shared and widely distributed because neither patient nor analyst can be identified. In addition, the database can be easily manipulated and studied by any-one using a personal computer.

The Open Access Project is based on three computer programs. Our first program, Data Extractor, extracts words and phrases from psychoanalytic texts and counts individual words and repeated word-sequences used by both patients and analysts. The program also tallies and stores eleven constant and countable variables—”counted items”— found in every psychoanalytic transcript. Data Extractor yields data based on two units of measure—entire psychoanalytic sessions and uninterrupted speech segments. The word tables and counted items are referred to as extracted data because they are extracted from transcripts that can be safely shared.

Data Explorer, our second program, allows analysts to easily focus on the frequency of specific words or phrases in the extracted data. For example, analysts interested in a patient's use of personal pronouns, such as you and I, can quickly obtain a count of these pronouns in each hour (or in each uninterrupted segment of speech). We cannot yet explain these observations, but we know they are facts that can now be checked and further studied.

Our poster focuses on an 80-hour sample of ten consecutive transcribed hours from each year of Mrs. C's 1,114-hour psycho-analysis. Mrs. C repeated the phrase “you know” a total of 1,251 times in our sample hours—4% in the first ten hours of her analysis, 22% in the next to the last year, and 74% in the final few weeks. These interesting facts about this much-studied case can now be checked and further studied.

Our final program, Data Analyzer, provides statistical summaries of the spreadsheets produced by Data Extractor and Data Explorer. The statistical summaries include mean, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, variance, t score, and R2. (An “Explanation of Summary Statistics Used with the Open Access Database” is included in the Documentation for Using Data Analyzer and Data Explorer.)

responseSet.authorMast: Steven P. Roose,  Suzanne Yang,  Eve Caligor,  Deborah L. Cabaniss,  Bruce Luber,  Justine Donovan,  Paul Rosen and  Nicholas R. Forand

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Posttermination Contact

A Survey Of Prevalence, Characteristics, And Analyst Attitudes

Steven Roose , Suzanne Yang , Eve Caligor , Deborah Cabaniss , Bruce Luber , Justine Donovan , Paul Rosen and Nicholas Forand

Nicholas Forand

1051 Riverside Drive
New York, NY 10032
Fax: 212-543-5607
E-mail: spr@pop.columbia.edu

Nicholas Forand  

Psychoanalysts believe that psychological work by the patient after termination of analysis consolidates and extends gains made during treatment. However, there is controversy over whether further contact bet-ween patient and analyst is appropriate. Some argue that if a patient returns to see the analyst it signifies an incomplete treatment, whereas others maintain that after termination a therapeutic relationship endures

455

and that patients can therefore benefit from recontacting their analysts. Systematic data on posttermination contact are limited.

Questionnaires were distributed to active graduates of the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research inquiring about the analyst's three most recently terminated cases that met the following criteria: the patient was in analysis; the analysis ended, for any reason, at least one year prior to the survey; and the case was not a training analysis (though control cases from the analyst's training could be included).

Results

Sixty percent (109/181) of analysts returned the survey, with 87 providing data on 241 cases. The median age of the patients was 31-40 years, and 42% percent were male. Eighty-three percent of cases were conducted at a frequency of four times a week, and 97% used the couch. Thirty-one percent of patients took psychotropic medication at some point during the analysis, and analysts prescribed the medication in 67% of these cases. Of the 241 cases, 22% continued for less than three years, 54% for three to six years, and 25% for more than six years; the median duration of an analysis was between four and five years.

In 87% of cases, regular contact ended at the termination of the analysis; in these cases, the possibility of posttermination contact was raised by 78% of the analysands. Eighty-seven percent of analysts let their patients know they could be available in the future.

Of the 210 cases in which regular contact ended with termination, 35% of patients had no subsequent contact, 25% percent had contact by phone, letter, or e-mail only, and 40% returned to see the analyst in person. Among analysands who returned to see their analyst, 35% came for a single office visit, 55% had extended consultations or began psychotherapy, and 5% resumed analysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creative Alliances

Enhancing Interfaces Between Psychoanalysis and Research

Hauser Stuart

Hauser Stuart

Hauser Stuart  

In this first research-oriented section of 2004, we begin a new tradition for JAPA—the regular publication of papers, abstracts, and letters designed to acquaint our readers with contemporary research initiatives relevant to current questions in psychoanalysis. These questions arise out of psychoanalytic theory, education, and clinical practice. Reflecting the spectrum of psychoanalytic topics, each section will aim to incorporate state-of-the-art research approaches from all relevant disciplines—social science, neuroscience, the humanities, and psychiatry. While this new section surely builds on several research issues that have appeared on an annual basis in the last three years (JAPA 49/1, 50/2, 51/3), it will differ with respect to its more frequent appearance in future volumes and in our commitment to initiate—through outstanding contemporary papers and other contributions—lively dialogue among clinicians, scholars, and educators who read both JAPA and literature from allied disciplines. Besides focusing on the “enduring dialectic between body and mind” (Mayes 2003, p. 745), we will attend to other crucial interfaces between contexts and individual development; narrative, ethnographic and quantitative approaches; and psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. Each of these interfaces points to many rich philosophical and scientific problems. Through our addressing the range of compelling ideas underlying these dialectics, psychoanalytic scholars and practitioners will attain new clarity on contemporary psychoanalytic controversies while discovering creative collaborations with scientists and scholars from related fields.

Emblematic of the intellectual excitement now surrounding psychoanalytic topics are the three subsections included here: (1)

385

responseSet.title: Treating Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Urban African American Mental Health Patients
responseSet.authorMast: Ann Schwartz,  Rebekah Bradley,  Kerry Ressler,  Melissa Sexton and  Alissa Sherry
responseSet.authorCitation: Schwartz, A., Bradley, R., Ressler, K., Sexton, M. & Sherry, A.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Treating Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Urban African American Mental Health Patients

Ann Schwartz , Rebekah Bradley , Kerry Ressler , Melissa Sexton and Alissa Sherry

Alissa Sherry

80 Jesse Hill Jr. Drive
P.O. Box 26238
Atlanta, GA 30303
E-mail: aschwa2@emory.edu

Alissa Sherry  

African American individuals in the urban community are at high risk for experiencing or witnessing violence and traumatic events, and therefore are at high risk for the development of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD). Despite this high risk, we have found that PTSD tends to be under-diagnosed and therefore undertreated in this population. This study aims to define the prevalence and comorbidity of PTSD at the outpatient mental health service of a large inner-city hospital in Atlanta, Georgia.

Method

We have screened approximately two hundred patients at random. We find that urban African American mental health patients have been extremely traumatized, with a remarkably high prevalence of PTSD (∼80% experienced severe trauma). Further, PTSD is grossly under-diagnosed in this population (the prevalence is 35-40%, compared to chart diagnosis of 5-10%). Compared with other mental health patients, those with PTSD are significantly more likely to have a history of suicide attempts or substance abuse and to be homeless. Based on the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-IV (SCID-I) data, those with PTSD are also more likely to have multiple comorbidities, primarily substance abuse and affective disorders, than those without PTSD. Finally, our findings that the majority of these patients have multiple traumatic experiences and are more likely to report an unstable early family life suggest that “Complex PTSD” may be highly prevalent. In summary, our data suggest that untreated PTSD may be a significant and often unrecognized source of morbidity within this population.

A Newly Constructed Intervention

In an attempt to optimize existing interventions, we have created a time-limited, manualized, culturally competent group therapy that integrates cognitive/behavioral and interpersonal approaches for use with a low-income, inner-city African American population.

464

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Patterns of Schizophrenic Language, Analyzed by Computerized Content Analysis

Yuko Hanakawa

Yuko Hanakawa

341 West 47th Street #1F
New York, NY 10036
E-mail: yukohanakawa@yahoo.com

Yuko Hanakawa  

The present project is a reanalysis of data used by Alpert et al. (2000) in a study comparing acoustic measures and content analysis of schizophrenic speech. Their study employed flat affect schizophrenic, non-flat affect schizophrenic, and nonclinical control groups. They found that acoustic analysis differentiated the flat affect group from non-flat affect and nonclinical samples in fluency and inflection, but that content analysis, based on the use of happy and sad word-categories, found no significant group differences.

This project examines the same data using computerized measures of Referential Activity, which assess the degree of connection between nonverbal experiences, including affect, and verbal expression (Bucci et al. 1992; Mergenthaler and Bucci 1999), and using word-lists that capture the phases of the referential process (Bucci 1997). The computerized linguistic measures were applied to the spoken language of schizophrenic and nonclinical participants using the Discourse Attribute Analysis Program, or DAAP (Maskit, Bucci, and Roussos 2002)

Richard M. Gottlieb Responds

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

263 West End Avenue 1-E
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: rmgottlieb@aol.com

Richard Gottlieb  

June 8, 2003. I am delighted that my psychoanalytic hypothesis regarding the therapeutic action of SSRI medications, published in JAPA 50/3, has stimulated further scholarly and clinical dialogue. To me, of the five responses printed here, the most intriguing and important is Levinson's. Since I view his comments as fundamentally misguided, I will devote more space to their discussion than to the more carefully reasoned, well-informed contributions of Ostow, Wolpert, Sandberg, and Levine. Ostow, Wolpert, and Levine are basically in agreement with me and bring their wealth of accumulated knowledge and experience to bear on my aggression hypothesis. Sandberg offers an intelligent, scientifically respectable alternative to my hypothesis, one which, in my opinion, merits further careful investigation. I will discuss these contributors shortly, but I feel I need most urgently to speak to Levinson's contentions.

493

[[RunningHead]]

The Feasibility Of Doing Empirical Research in the Psychoanalytic Training Clinic

Margaret Hamilton , Eve Caligor , Verna MacCornack and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

170 West End Avenue, #1E
New York, NY 10023
E-Mail: mis3@columbia.edu

Steven Roose  

Margaret S. Hamilton, Eve Caligor, Verna MacCornack, and Steven P. Roose (Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research)

In a recent article, Gabbard, Gunderson, and Fonagy (2002) propose a program for a multisite study in which all analytic control cases at participating institutes become subjects in a rudimentary outcome study. While these authors make a strong case for the desirability of such a project, they do not address the question of feasibility. Earlier work (Vaughan et al. 2000) looking at the feasibility of doing research in the analytic clinic of the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research concluded that the research was negatively impacted due to low recruitment rates. The researchers hypothesized that the low recruitment rate in the psychoanalytic clinic may have stemmed from several factors, including a lack of conviction about the relevance of research and concern about the impact of the research on the treatment process among the psychoanalytic clinicians.

CHARACTER TRANSFORMATION THROUGH THE PSYCHOTHERAPEUTIC RELATIONSHIP. By Robert E. Hooberman. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 2002, 320 pp., $50.00.

Mardi Horowitz

Mardi Horowitz

401 Parnassus Avenue
Box 0984 (Room 346)
San Francisco, CA 94143
E-mail: mardi@itsa.ucsf.edu

Mardi Horowitz   Professor of Psychiatry, University of California, San Francisco.

This is an excellent book, in its genre. The genre I refer to is books written by psychoanalysts who, after decades of work, integrate their clinical experience with existing theory. They summarize what they were taught, quote subsequent literature, and put together a revision and consolidation that they have found valuable. The books in this genre cover the gamut of theory and technique as applied to personality change. The best books are helpful to experienced clinicians and, more important, to trainees. This is such a book.

Hooberman reviews the literature central to American ego psychology and extends himself to include object relations, self psychological, and intersubjective works. Striving toward synthesis, he theorizes about how character is modified in the course of therapy-centered relationship experiences, as affected by transference interpretation, countertransference, and the recollection of childhood experiences.

While the author deals with various characterological types, he does not segregate them in specific chapters. Instead, he regards each individual as a unique character, to be discovered only to a degree, and with great difficulty, in the context of psychoanalytic treatment.

1279

NEW CLINICAL REALMS: PUSHING THE ENVELOPE OF THEORY AND TECHNIQUE. By Salman Akhtar. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 2003, xvi + 283 pp., $45.00.

Herbert Gomberg

Herbert Gomberg

5924 Royal Lane, Suite 260
Dallas, TX 75230
E-mail: lhgomberg@earthlink.net

Herbert Gomberg   Training and Supervising Analyst, Dallas Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Texas-Southwestern Medical School.

This is Salman Akhtar's fifth book. In his bibliography, he cites twentytwo papers he has written and nine others written with coauthors. These

1281

occupy two pages of a 32-page list of references, covering a wide range of psychoanalytic, psychiatric, and cultural (especially poetic) interests. The book consists of ten articles, four previously published in psychoanalytic journals, three in psychoanalytic or psychiatric books, and three not previously published. The book is organized into five parts of two papers each, all dealing with problems that have caught Akhtar's interest and that he feels have received too little attention. Under the rubric unexplored problems are chapters on mental pain and on writer's block; under unrecognized personalities, “The Shy Narcissist” and “The Other Woman”; under uncelebrated partners, chapters on animals and on things; under unfamiliar processes, chapters on dynamic and developmental process; and under untapped potentials, chapters on mentorship and on forgiveness. All are psychoanalytically informed, though some are more psychiatric than psychoanalytic in style.

These essays are the products of Akhtar's serious scholarship, thought, and hard work on some difficult topics. His wide-ranging knowledge and exploration of the literature relating to these topics, from Freud to the current views of diverse theorists and clinicians—with an accent on developmental aspects and cultural factors, including poetry and the dislocation of immigration—give his work its particular tone.

In his preface, Akhtar presents an excellent brief account of each chapter; I will not review all of them. In several of these essays, notably “Writer's Block,” “The Shy Narcissist,” “Animals,” and “Forgiveness,” Akhtar reviews the literature, adds his own perspective, and discusses the subject's various clinical manifestations. Analysts interested in the subject will find considerable material to further their understanding. The chapter on the other woman is based largely on Akhtar's clinical experience with a number of such women seen in consultation, two of whom he treated in some depth. He has useful things to say about the diagnostic and dynamic distinctions between the two main groups he distinguishes—and also about treatment issues, including countertransference problems. In his chapter on mental pain, he opts for a narrow definition; he speaks not of all painful affects but of a particularly painful, “somewhat unknowable feeling of despair, longing, and psychic helplessness” (p. 5) in response to a loss that is experienced as a loss of part of the self. He discusses the various defensive maneuvers used to deal with such pain and finds both the potentially pathological and the potentially adaptive aspects of each.

[[RunningHead]]

Sylvia Brody on the Development of Anorexia Nervosa

Bordy Sylvia

Bordy Sylvia

1148 Fifth Avenue
New York, NY 10128

Bordy Sylvia  

August 5, 2003. Nathan Szajnberg's review of my book, The Development of Anorexia Nervosa: The Hunger Artists (JAPA 51/2), includes many misunderstandings of the text. I shall cite a few of them.

Several times he refers to my research as having been prospective, which it never was. When I began the work I had no thought of trying to determine the outcome of early experience. I offered no hypothesis about what my future observations might disclose. In view of a previous project concerned with observations of young infants in the presence of their mothers, in which I had participated in Topeka, Kansas, from 1948 to 1950, I wished mainly to enlarge our understanding of psychological events in the oral phase. Mark Twain once said that everybody talks about the weather, but nobody does anything about it; similarly, I had come to realize that “everybody” wrote or spoke about the oral phase but few had studied its complexities—the day-to-day, hour-to-hour, or moment-to-moment events it encompasses, and their possible effects on the infant's and child' s ongoing behavior and development.

In 1963 I started to collect direct observations and running films of the behavior of a sample of mothers (N = 131) engaged in the care of their infants, and so began to learn from those mothers what they were able and willing to tell about their experience with the observed infant. I realized that how a mother fed—in what physical positions, with what affects, with what knowledge—was often more important than what she fed.

Szajnberg accepts a common assumption that anorexia appears in adolescence, so skipping the salient fact—reported in my book— that the eating disorders of the two subjects began to emerge in infancy. One of the two mothers noticed signs of the disorder during her daughter's childhood, but it was never diagnosed as such. More important, Szajnberg omits the main thrust of my account, which reports the slow, silent, and oppressive development of anorexia over the years.

He tells the reader to read this book “above all” to look at the girls' drawings. The emphasis is misplaced: the drawings are illustrative, but in themselves do not tell the essential story.

[[RunningHead]]

Nathan Szajnberg Responds

Nathan Szajnberg

Nathan Szajnberg

1720 Lake Street
San Francisco, CA 94121
E-mail: nmoshe@earthlink.net

Nathan Szajnberg  

September 10, 2003. I have admired Sylva Brody's work for years, feel privileged to have been asked to continue the thirty-year follow-up, and read her latest book with deep interest and knowledge of at least one of the two subjects. I take full responsibility for what I wrote. I tried not to reveal my strong bias for her work, as I thought that that would undermine the credibility of the review. My review could have been brief: read this important study. But I felt an obligation to recount the overall themes of the book.

Brody writes that I accept the “assumption that anorexia appears in adolescence.” To the contrary, I make two major points in the review: (1) her book suggests “a new view of anorexia nervosa as a developmental pathology” with “adverse mothering in infancy as a strain trauma” and “puberty as a secondary trauma” (p. 706), and (2) it shows anorexia to be “but the most torrid manifestation of a lifelong misery that waxes and wanes” (p. 706).

I say in my review that Brody (to my knowledge) has given us the strongest evidence that anorexia nervosa is a lifelong developmental disorder associated with adverse mothering in infancy that is perhaps more floridly manifested as “anorexia nervosa” in phenomenological terms in adolescence. This is a profound contribution. I note in my review that the prevailing belief (at least until this book) has been that anorexia nervosa is a disorder of adolescence. I do this to make the more significant comment about Brody's book.

Brody gives us evidence that an otherwise “normative event,” puberty and its psychological garb, adolescence, can serve as a traumatic experience in someone who is vulnerable because of early strain trauma (in this case, in infancy and around the mother-child interaction, including feeding). This is a major contribution.

1301

Art History and Psychoanalysis

Taylor Sue

Taylor Sue

P.O. Box 751
Portland, OR 97207
Fax: 503-725-4541

Taylor Sue  

December 12, 2003. I am grateful to JAPA for the review by Janice Lieberman of my book, Hans Bellmer: The Anatomy of Anxiety (JAPA 51/4), and for the opportunity to respond. There are several errors in the review, which I would like to correct. Bellmer (1902-1975) was German, not Polish; his birthplace, the town of Kattowitz, became part of Poland only after World War I. The book's cover image, Ball Joint (1936), displays not the red bloody vagina Lieberman describes between two doll's arms but, perhaps just as strange, a large eyeball with a blue iris. It was in 1923, not 1913, that Bellmer went to Berlin with his father to study engineering. The second ball-jointed doll he created in the 1930s had two pelvises, not three. And when he divorced his second wife in 1946, he was separated from his twin daughters, not stepdaughters as Lieberman reports.

These factual inaccuracies are accompanied by several misstatements about my interpretive positions. I do not dispute that Bellmer hated his father, as he loudly proclaimed; instead, I acknowledge this conscious hatred while proposing, in addition, a repressed erotic attachment. Nor do I refute Susan Suleiman's argument that in surrealism the female body is an accessory in the male artist's oedipal skirmishes with the father. On the contrary, I embrace this thesis enthusiastically and build upon it. (The title of her book is Subversive Intent, not Subversive Thought, as Lieberman lists it in her references.) Although I do use the classical concept of the fetish as phallic substitute in my discussion of Bellmer's doll, I also invoke the contemporary view of the preoedipal origins of fetishism, so effectively summarized by Donald Kuspit (1993) in his essay “The Modern Fetish.” Finally, I suggest a return to the primal scene not, as Lieberman states, in Bellmer's graphic photographs of female genitals, but in several late drawings and prints that depict the artist himself having sex with two children.

1303

Janice S. Lieberman Responds

Janice Lieberman

Janice Lieberman

55 East 87th Street
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: janicelieberman@compuserve.com

Janice Lieberman  

February 2, 2004. I welcome the opportunity to respond to Sue Taylor's letter. I appreciate her asking for clarification of the difference between a clinical psychoanalyst and an art historian in the examination of the relationship between the art and the psyche of an artist, in this case Hans Bellmer. I want her to know that I admire her book from an art historical point of view, for she presents Bellmer's entire oeuvre rather than just his early works, and that is of great interest and value.

[[RunningHead]]

Change After Long-Term Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy

Alexander Wilczek , Jacques Barber , J. Gustavsson , Marie Åsberg and Robert Weinryb

Robert Weinryb

Bjöårdsgatan 25
S-118 52 Stockholm
SWEDEN
E-mail: alexander.wilczek@pi.ki.se

Robert Weinryb   Department of Clinical Neuroscience, Psychotherapy Section, Karolinska Institutet.

Psychoanalytic psychotherapy in clinical practice is traditionally a longterm treatment conducted by well-trained psychotherapists. However, very few studies have been published that evaluate the effects of such treatment. To redress this lack of studies, 55 individuals selected for longterm psychoanalytic psychotherapy (average, 3 years) were invited to participate in a naturalistic study. The psychotherapists had a mean of 15 years of professional experience. The 36 patients who completed psychotherapy manifested a substantial reduction in symptomatic suffering and decreased levels of character pathology, as measured by the Karolinska Psychodynamic Profile (KAPP) and the Karolinska Scales of Personality. Generally, such changes were not found in the individuals who did not engage in treatment. In the therapy group, improvements were found on eight KAPP subscales defining different aspects of character: Intimacy and Reciprocity, Frustration Tolerance, Regression in the Service of the Ego, Coping with Aggressive Affects, Conceptions of Bodily Appearance and their Significance for Self-esteem, Sexual Function, Sexual Satisfaction, and Personality Organization. The results indicate that individuals who engaged in psychotherapy improved their capacity to handle crucial aspects of life and reduced their symptomatic suffering.

Not the Thing I was

Thirteen Years at Bruno Bettelheim's Orthogenic School. By Stephen Eliot. New York: St. Martin's Press, 2002, 288 pp., $24.95.

Michael Beldoch

Michael Beldoch

310 West 72nd Street
New York, NY 10023
Fax: 212-787-9413
E-mail: michaelbeldoch@verizon.net

Michael Beldoch   Clinical Professor of Psychology in Psychiatry, Weill Medical College, Cornell University; Attending Psychologist, New York Presbyterian Hospital; member, Institute for the History of Psychiatry, Weill Medical College.

It is more than ten years since Bruno Bettelheim ended his life in a plastic bag and unleashed a torrent of extremist opinion about the nature of the man. The author of sixteen books, Bettelheim in the midnineties was the subject of two full-length biographies in English that described him in diametrically opposite terms. Now, after what one of those biographers called “the Bettelheim affair” has disappeared from written view, another kind of biography altogether has been published, a first-person narrative of thirteen years spent as a student/patient in the Sonia Shankman Orthogenic School of the University of Chicago, more popularly known as Bruno Bettelheim's School.

The author, now an investment banker approaching fifty, relied not only on his memory of those years, but from sections of the over one thousand pages of transcribed notes sent to him at his request in 1992 by the director at the time. Nevertheless, Eliot reports that he did not look at those notes for another eight years after he received them, writing the first 270 pages from memory alone. The book includes a special section with excerpts from the notes, dictated by staff, which make for interesting reading.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Humanities

The Preference for the Primitive: Episodes in the History of Western Taste and Art. By E.H. Gombrich. London: Phaidon Press, 2002, 324 pp., $59.95.

Martin Bergmann

Martin Bergmann

1136 Fifth Avenue
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-427-4947

Martin Bergmann   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Freudian Society; Professor of Psychology, Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University.

The late and distinguished art historian Erich H. Gombrich was no stranger to psychoanalysis. In 1953 he presented to the British Psychoanalytical Society the Ernest Jones lecture, “Psychoanalysis and the History of Art,” in which the psychoanalytic idea of regression was first applied to the understanding of art. In 1966 he published in the magazine Encounter an article titled “Freud's Aesthetics,” in which he explained Freud's disdain for modern art. Gombrich found an answer to the question of why Freud saw himself as the patron saint of surrealism and at the same time was unsympathetic to surrealist works. The regression that Freud would tolerate in a work of art has to be under the control of the ego. Surrealist regression was judged by Freud as having gone too far and therefore being insufficiently under the ego's control.

In this posthumously published book, Gombrich considers the problem of regression at greater length and in greater depth. Already in the Jones Lecture he noted that “impressionism succeeded in excluding literary association and in confining the give and take to the reading of the scrambled colour-patches. But in return for this effort of shared activity, it yields a wonderful premium of regressive pleasure. For the first time in several centuries the public were allowed to see real splashes of loud, bright, luminous colours which had been banned as too crude and primitive by Academic convention” (Gombrich 1953, p. 41). By “literary association” Gombrich means the adding of a biblical or mythological context to landscapes, as if landscape itself was not enough of a topic.

The brief analysis of impressionism is remarkable for two reasons.

301

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Perceived Parental Cultural Conflict and the Psychosocial Adjustment of Arab American College Students

Hani Henry and Mia Biran

Mia Biran

122 Benton Hall
Oxford, OH 45056
E-mail: Biranmw@muohio.edu

Mia Biran  

Conflicts between the old culture and the new one can profoundly affect immigrants' lives, with consequent impact on their families. Akhtar (1999) argued that immigrants may experience intrapsychic turmoil reminiscent of the individuation process of adolescence. Similarly, Grinberg and Grinberg (1984) suggested that immigration can trigger anxiety due to the failure to discriminate between the old and the new, with profound and lasting effects. Immigrants may feel inundated with chaotic messages and may suffer from cultural shock. Immigrants may also be conflicted because of their fear of loss of identity. Such cultural conflicts, when experienced by immigrant parents, may consequently affect the psychological and social functioning of their children. For example, Hyman, Yu, and Beiser (2000) noted that for immigrants to America from more collectivist Southeast Asian cultures, the incompatibility of Western values, which emphasize self-fulfillment, and the traditional pattern of subordinating the self to the interests of the family can be a source of internal conflict for immigrant youth.

Method

This study examined a range of behaviors immigrant Arab parents adopt in relation to their cultural conflicts and how these behaviors may be related both to their control of their children and their children's subjective well-being. We used a newly constructed measure, Perceived Parental Cultural Conflict Scale (PPCCS). This measure includes two independent subscales that assess the degree to which the respondent's immigrant parents are perceived as oriented toward (a) preservation of the old culture (Preservation subscale) and (b) openness to the American culture (Openness subscale). Difference scores between these two subscales represent the degree to which parents are perceived as conflicted with, or separated from, the American culture (e.g., a negative score would indicate inclined assimilation into the American culture). We also used a measure of perceived parental control and autonomy, the Autonomy Control Scale (de Man 1987), and an Index of Well Being (Campbell, Converse, and Rodgers 1976).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Information Feedback Theory of the Functions of Emotions and an Empirically Testable System for their Classification

Hartvig Dahl

Hartvig Dahl

Box 88
450 Clarkson Avenue
Brooklyn, NY 11203
E-mail: dahl@bmec.hscbklyn.edu

Hartvig Dahl  

If we are ever to have a general theory of how the psychotherapeutic process works, it is clear that such a theory must be based on a widely accepted theory of emotions. But it is equally apparent that at the present time there is no general acceptance of even a few competing theories. This situation is particularly puzzling, since it would be hard to find a psychoanalyst who does not regard the patient's and the analyst's emotions (affects) as fundamental to understanding their communications, particularly the content of the stories patients tell about their lives, their childhood experiences, and their relationship with the analyst.

It is further puzzling because Freud (1915) provided what he regarded as the three dimensions basic to all mental life: SubjectObject, Pleasure-Unpleasure, and Active-Passive. These are the basis of the classification system offered here. Figure 1 shows a threedimensional tree with examples of emotions for each of the eight resulting categories. As Freud emphasized, the Subject-Object dimension (here called IT-ME) is preeminent because it implies the crucial distinction between ourselves and the objects we interact with. In empirical studies, when subjects are given large lists of emotion words to classify on these dimensions, they are able to do so with reliabilities in the high nineties on each dimension. The dimensions are completely uncorrelated with one another (i.e., they are independent). The display will show many additional facts about the classifications.

The information feedback theory of the functions of emotions emphasizes the crucial distinction between the functions of the IT and the ME emotions. The theory is based on the concept of IT emotions as wishes and ME emotions as beliefs about their outcome. Figure 2 illustrates the theory graphically, with two different outcomes. The theory regards emotional wishes as having the crucial characteristics of appetitive wishes that require consummatory behaviors to satisfy them. If the wish can be consummated, the outcome is a belief (a positive ME emotion) experienced as Pleasure; if the wish cannot be consummated, the outcome is a belief experienced as Unpleasure (a negative ME emotion), commonly with associated defenses.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Strange Bedfellows

Relationship Satisfaction and Domestic Violence

Robert Waldinger , Daniel Fishman , Cynthia Moore and Laura Chivers

Laura Chivers

1249 Boylston Street 3rd floor
Boston, MA 02115
Fax: 815-377-6637
E-mail: rwaldinger@partners.org

Laura Chivers  

More than 25 percent of all couple relationships include physical aggression at some point. Psychological aggression between partners is more prevalent—and potentially more harmful—than physical aggression

478

between intimate partners. This study compared the relationship between physical and psychological aggression and relationship satisfaction in violent and nonviolent couples.

Method

The sample consisted of 58 heterosexual, English-speaking couples, married or living together for over a year. Couples were recruited by community advertisements, and their ethnic makeup reflects that of the greater Boston area. To ensure a range of violence in our sample, couples were screened using a modified version of the Conflict Tactics Scale (Straus and Gelles 1990). The frequencies of physical assault and psychological aggression were assessed using the Revised Conflict Tactics Scale (Straus et al. 1996), and marital satisfaction was assessed using the Locke-Wallace Short Marital Adjustment Test (Locke and Wallace 1959). Out of 73 people with physically violent partners, 35 reported being satisfied with their relationships. To better understand this phenomenon, we used multiple regression analyses to examine whether the experience of physical or of psychological aggression was more strongly related to relationship satisfaction.

Results and Discussion

Compared with women who were dissatisfied with physically violent partners, women who were satisfied in their relationships with such partners experienced lower levels of psychological aggression. Women's psychological aggression was related to men's dissatisfaction with their relationships, although women's physical aggression was not. These findings bear out clinical observations that intimidation and the constant threat of violence can be more stressful to women than actual physical violence itself.

Edward Wolpert  

April 21, 2003. Richard Gottlieb has made much of the ability of SSRI medications to modify aggression and has elevated this observation to the status of an hypothesis. There is no clinical doubt that SSRIs can modify aggression in some patients, and that such a modification is also found to be true with older antidepressants, such as tricyclics and monoamine oxidase inhibitors. Indeed, the success of any member of any class of antidepressants depends on its ability to modify aggression in the given depressed patient. What has so far eluded psychopharmacologists is the ability to predict which antidepressant will work in which patient. Some mood stabilizers, such as lithium carbonate and depakote, are also able to modify aggression in ways that prevent the recurrence of both mania and depression in bipolar patients. Such observations suggest that affective disorders generally are diseases of dysregulation of energy: too much or too little in the system clinically representing mania on the one hand, or depression on the other. With this broadened hypothesis, one is able to explain why some patients seem to have affective disorders based on the length of time light strikes the retina, others based on a discontinuity between external and internal clocking systems, and still others based on shifts of internal self-regard. The initiating agent in each case may differ, but the end result is an affective disorder with similar end-state symptoms, regardless of initiating event.

The broadening of Gottlieb's SSRI hypothesis would lead to the research he suggests, as well as to an approach to affective states that is even broader in scope, embracing affective states determined entirely by physiological factors, others whose determination is purely psychological, and still others with contributions from both physiological and psychological parameters.

489

[[RunningHead]]

On the Therapeutic Action of SSRI Medications

Frederic Levine

Frederic Levine

7600 Red Road
Suite 108
Miami, FL 33143-5421
E-mail: fredericjl@aol.com

Frederic Levine  

December 8, 2002. Richard Gottlieb's stimulating discussion of the effects of SSRIs, in which he argues that the demonstrable efficacy of these medications in such seemingly unrelated disorders as depression, social anxiety, and obsessive-compulsive disorder is explainable by the proposition that “the essential benefits of these medications derive from their capacity to modify aggression,” is an unfortunately all-too-rare instance of the way that psychoanalytic thinking “out of the box” can productively be applied to other disciplines, and to matters that transcend analysts' doctrinal arguments and specialized clinical tasks. His line of thought, if extended by the sorts of research that he suggests, gives hope that, someday, psychiatric diagnosis may move beyond sterile catalogues of behavior that are clinically valueless, and often shackling to our students, toward a classification that reflects essential commonalities and processes in the mind.

Gottlieb's use of analytic thinking away from the couch has much in common, in principle, with the great research applications of psychoanalytic ideas that were once such an impressive part of academic psychology and anthropology—as by Adorno, Bruner, Erdelyi, and others—and that practitioners in various medical disciplines once found so useful in understanding psychosomatic interactions. It is a refreshing sign that we're not dead yet, still relevant, and I think he deserves thanks from all of us for that.

There is a next step that might now be taken—which is to ask more about the modification of aggression brought about by SSRIs. What is the mechanism of their action? Do they directly affect aggression on a CNS level? Could there be an indirect effect, as on the motor channels that express aggression? After all, in most of the syndromes that he cites, and in most of those that we encounter in daily practice, the pathogenic conflict centers on the patient's fear of his or her aggression, the sense of being potentially dangerous and monstrous toward others, and not on excessively violent actions. This might be further illuminated by studies of the effects of SSRIs on populations of truly violent individuals who feel they are out of control. And there is the related question of whether the benefits of any other psychotropic medications may derive from their effects on aggression or other impulses.

Luisa Busch de Ahumada

Luisa Busch de Ahumada   Associate Member, Buenos Aires Psychoalytic Society; Instructor, Postgraduate Course on Child Psychiatry, Buenos Aires University School of Medicine.

The emerging psychoanalytic avant garde, vouchsafes the philosopherpsychologist Carlo Strenger in the final lines of this book, is characterized by the celebration of individuality rather than the pathologization of differences. Renouncing any claim to knowledge as illegitimate and unrrealistic, he pursues a radical narrativization taking constructivism one step further. Arguing that the tasks of individuality have changed greatly since the beginning of the twentieth century and that heterosexuality has gradually lost its claims, what was once transgressive is now a cultural alternative in lifestyle, while social norms are no longer accepted as necessary or “natural.” His overall goal, he avows (p. 32), is to radicalize the constructivist approach along Nietzschean and Foucauldian lines.

Holding to an all-out epistemic nihilism, he maintains that “it has been one of the persistent illusions of our profession that we can arrive at definitive formulations of the deep structures of the mind on the basis of clinical observation” (p. 193). At most, he says, we can formulate narratives. He concedes that his descriptions do not much differentiate between psychoanalytic and other approaches (p. 60). In his view, psychoanalytic theory making has been mythological without knowing it, and “what happens to myth when it becomes conscious of its own nature is that it becomes literature” (p. 63). Not fitting his idea of science, modeled on the “hard sciences”—scientific theories are to him formalized (p. 73) and algorithmic (p. 134), while psychoanalytic ones are metaphoric or mythical creations—psychoanalysis should be understood as a literary enterprise under the postmodern paradigm of art, which from romanticism on has been governed by the ideal of new creation, rather than that of science (p. 51).

947

Jeremy Mack

Jeremy Mack

10 East 96th Street
New York, NY 10128

Jeremy Mack   Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry, NYU School of Medicine; Lecturer, Mount Sinai School of Medicine.

In Affect Intolerance in Patient and Therapist, Stanley Coen describes the predicament of patients who experience this condition and the situation of the therapist who attempts to help them. The book draws on and reworks issues Coen has dealt with in previous articles and books and adds new material. Coen's overall conclusion is that a patient's inability or unwillingness to tolerate affect requires, of the therapist who hopes to effect change, alterations in expectations and an exquisite ability to tolerate strong affects himor herself. “It is a major premise of this book,” says Coen, “that both patients and therapists have difficulty tolerating intense affects, loving and hating, and a central aim of this book is to help both to do so …”; therapists must be prepared to bear what their patients cannot, and the “therapist's most essential task is to persist in feeling with and for patients their terrors of their own feelings and of their needs for the therapist” (pp. xxii, xxv). The patient will not be able to tolerate these affects if the therapist cannot.

The book is divided into three parts: What Makes Affect Intolerable for Patient and Analyst?; What Is the Therapist's Role in Helping the Patient Develop Affect Tolerance?; and Helping Therapists' Affect Tolerance through Talking and Writing about Our Work. In discussing barriers to love in the first chapter, “Barriers to Love between Patient and Analyst,” Coen points in blame to the therapeutic ego ideal of most analysts, which remains intolerant and critical of the analyst's having loving and hating feelings toward patients.

957

One South Avenue

Wilma Bucci  

This study attempted to look beneath the self-report measure of depression (BDI) using clinical/projective data and language style data in a nonclinical sample of university students by addressing three research questions. The first question focused on the relationship between self reported depression using the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) and Referential Activity (RA;see Bucci and Mergenthaler 1999) measures ap plied to early memories. Contrary to previous work with clinically de pressed subjects, the expected relationship of low RA to depression was not found.

Luborsky's Core Conflictual Relationship Theme, or CCRT (Luborsky and Crits-Christoph 1990), was applied to the early memories for a subset of participants to examine possible underlying dynamics that can not be seen through a self-report. Based on this method, the second research question, concerning differences between participants' CCRT responses of Self (RS) and Other (RO) focusing on the quality of the responses, positive vs. negative, was addressed.

Quality of Therapist Communications and Impact on Patients in Cognitive Behavioral and Psychodynamic Short-term Therapy using the Aps

Sherwood Waldron and Kenneth Winarick

Kenneth Winarick

1235 Park Avenue, #1B
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-722-3791
E-mail: woodywald@earthlink.net

Kenneth Winarick  

A cognitive behavior therapy and a matched short-term dynamic therapy from a pilot study led by Perry and Robertson (Trijsburg, Semeniuk, and Perry in press) were assessed using the Analytic Process Scales, or APS (Waldron et al. 2004a, b). Using an extensive coding manual, the APS facilitates the assessment of tape-recorded sessions, enabling the evaluation of the nature and quality of the contributions of analyst and patient to the psychoanalytic process and to study their interactions. Clinical psychology interns achieved good reliability, rating a total of 185 segments from tape recordings of an early and a late session from each case (mean “alpha” coefficient with three or four raters was.68), without requiring extensive training in the use of the APS.

The nature of the work by each therapist-patient pair was examined, showing marked differences between the two cases in “core analytic activities,” including clarifying, interpreting, addressing inner conflict, and addressing transference manifestations, in the expected direction. Although the CBT case showed substantially less of these core analytic activities by the therapist than did the psychodynamic case (approximately half as much of each of the core analytic activities), the levels of these activities in the CBT case were nevertheless substantial.

A regression analysis provided a confirmation of the findings of Waldronet al. (2004b) that the quality of the therapist's communication has a pivotal role in facilitating the patient's subsequent communication. The assessment of quality includes the aptness of the type of intervention, the usefulness of its content, and the skill of presentation, including tact, timing, and language appeal.

The facilitating role of quality applies both to the psychodynamic treatment and to CBT, a striking finding extending the findings of Ablon and Jones (1998). These authors found using a different instrument, the Psychodynamic Process Q-sort (PQS), that it was the psycho-dynamic elements, and not the cognitive behavioral elements, that were associated with patient improvement for both the psychodynamic and the CBT patients.

Psychoanalysis and Culture

EXPLORING THE INVISIBLE: ART, SCIENCE, AND THE SPIRITUAL. By Lynn Gamwell. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2002, 344 pp., $49.95.

Janice Coco

Janice Coco

Portland, OR 97219

Janice Coco   Visiting Professor, Department of Art, Lewis and Clark College, Portland, OR.

Lynn Gamwell's Exploring the Invisible: Art, Science, and the Spiritual is a handsomely appointed text, offering visual delight from the design of its cover through the profusion of carefully selected illustrations. In this, the author lays a feast for the eye and provides an engaging read as well. Gamwell, director of the art museum at the State University of New York, Binghamton, invites the reader in with a provocative question: “Mystery has shrouded abstract art since it emerged in the late nineteenth century. Where did it come from? How did shape, color, and line come to be the vocabulary of the modern painter?” (p. 9). In response, the author traces modernist abstraction to its tenable roots—the secularized, evolutionary worldview signaled by Darwin's On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection, published in 1859. With this, she shapes the book's premise into a study of abstract visual art, as affected by the modern scientific zeitgeist. Beginning with nineteenth-century romanticism, she demonstrates how artists plumbed scientific ideas, both microscopic and telescopic, to express visible and invisible realities. No mean undertaking, the thirteen chapters are devoted to the exploration of geology, evolution, the human mind, the science of color, Einsteinian relativity, quantum physics, and the nature of the atom. Indeed, each chapter holds the seed of another book (or several books) and draws on the more comprehensive studies of others. Cast in twenty-first-century idiom, Exploring the Invisible recalls in its grand scope the eighteenth-century Encyclopedia of the philosophe Denis Diderot—an Enlightenment text intended to chronicle the relationships between technological progress, society, and art.

1253

Reflections on Clinical Experience

THE ANALYST'S ANALYST WITHIN. By Laura Heims Tessman. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2003, viii + 363 pp., $57.50.

Jeffrey Golland

Jeffrey Golland

145 Fourth Avenue
New York, NY 10003

Jeffrey Golland   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Freudian Society; Professor, Department of Early Childhood and Childhood Education, Lehman College of the City University of New York.

This book offers even more than its engaging title promises. It addresses a central feature of what is therapeutic about psychoanalysis, and how the process may work. Questions about therapeutic action have generated considerable discourse, yet consensus remains elusive. Insight theory is challenged by reference to the power of the analytic object relationship. Interpretation competes as explanation with interactional experience. In a recent contribution, Gabbard and Westen (2003) propose a systematic view that postulates therapeutic actions rather than a singular action.

Laura Heims Tessman, in a work both ambitious and modest, addresses several questions related to therapeutic action. Her ambition is demonstrated by a research model appropriate both to the complexity of psychoanalytic process and to its essential subjectivity. Her modesty is shown by explicit acknowledgment of the limitations of subjectivity, especially of the particular subjects of this study, as well as by her goal of raising illuminating questions rather than trying, prematurely, to test hypotheses.

Tessman studied a specific facet of psychoanalytic action: the internalized effect of analyst on analysand following termination of treatment. Her subject “participants” were themselves analysts, who had of course been analysands. An open-ended interview format focused on participants' structured and free reflections. Tessman's aim, as her title suggests, was to understand how the analyst's own analyst continues to function within the mind of that analyst.

1269

[[RunningHead]]

Use Your Words!

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

Payne Whitney Clinic, Box 140
525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-746-5944
E-mail: tshapiro@med.cornell.edu

Theodore Shapiro   Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Medical College of Cornell University; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

“Use your words!” is a phrase admonishing preschoolers to divert their action-proneness to thought and language. Freud's injunction against acting out had a similar aim, placing control over drives in the domain of “inner language.” The twenty-first-century psychoanalyst continues to employ models that depend on mentalization viewed from two angles—neural inhibition and social discourse. Psychoanalysts bolster their position by borrowing from the basic scientific work in each area. The recent focus on enactments, intersubjectivity, and social constructivism is reconsidered from an historical vantage point, as is the work that seeks to reconcile recent findings in neuroimaging and cognitive neuroscience. Freud's vision included a holistic hope that a comprehensive science of human beings might be achieved by understanding derived from biological inquiry and the artifacts of social and cultural narratives. The author's experience in both domains is recounted, and a new reconciliation of disparate approaches is offered in linguistic complementarity.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalysts Past

Commitment and Compassion in Psychoanalysis: The Collected Papers ofEdward M. Weinshel. Edited by Robert M. Wallerstein. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2003, $59.95.

Shelley Orgel

Shelley Orgel

245 East 87th Street
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-628-3044
E-mail: shelleyorgel@aol.com

Shelley Orgel   Training and Supervising Analyst, NYU Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, NYU Medical Center.

This volume will be a generous, surprising gift to those meeting Edward M. Weinshel for the first time. His many former students and colleagues, and the friends he has made around the world, will be freshly reminded of the range and continuing vitality of his contri- butions to psychoanalytic thought. He has served our science and profession brilliantly and selflessly for more than thirty years.

The editor, Robert S. Wallerstein, a moving force behind the enterprise, has chosen six important clinical papers and eight theoretical ones, and has contributed incisive commentaries. The volume gains emotional resonance and poignancy from personal tributes and memories offered by Weinshel's wife Jenny, his daughter Julie Weinshel Tepper, his stepdaughter Dena Marshall Sorbo, and a granddaughter, Kaitlin Pittel Stark. Leonard Shengold, a close personal friend, colleague, and comrade-in-arms in the world of American and international psychoanalytic activities, creates an insightful portrait of Weinshel as a man with a gift for friendship, a generous mentor, a passionate advocate for his beliefs, and an upholder of standards of excellence in his beloved profession.

Several of the papers were written in collaboration with Weinshel's dear friend, the late Victor Calef. The two drew from one another the best each could offer; they were true brothers in psychoanalysis and in life.

Culture and Subjectivity

Aboriginal Populations in the mind. By Celia Brickman. New York: Columbia University Press, 2003, 293 pp., $59.50.

Robert Bergman

Robert Bergman

2271 NE 51st Street
Seattle, WA 98105
Fax: 206-522-7815
E-mail: rlbergman@comcast.net

Robert Bergman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Seattle Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; faculty, Northwest Center for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Washington.

This book is about Freud and the heart of darkness. Brickman takes her title from Freud's essay, “The Unconscious” (1915): “The content of the Ucs. may be compared to an aboriginal population in the mind” (p. 195). Six years before he wrote that, a fellow middle European wrote, “The Nellie, a cruising yawl, swung to her anchor without a flutter of the sails, and was at rest [in the estuary of the Thames]. … ‘and this also,' said Marlow suddenly, ‘has been one of the dark places of the earth'” (pp. 45, 48). Then, in narrating Heart of Darkness, Conrad (1902) goes on to tell a very dark story indeed. The idea that in Roman times the Thames was as dark as the Congo seems to imply that the darkness is due to the primitivity of the inhabitants, but in Marlow's account it is the Romans on the Thames and the Belgians on the Congo who are the real savages. A thin veneer of altruism, the supposed mission to enlighten the aborigines, was the excuse for looting, exploitation, and mass murder on a grand scale.

Hochschild (1998) has shown that though the book has been regarded as a fictional metaphor for all kinds of darkness of the heart, Conrad intended it as an exposé of genocide and that “whatever rich levels of meaning the book has as literature … [it is] a precise and detailed description of the actual facts of the case” (p. 143). There were real versions of Kurtz with real collections of severed heads decorating their gardens. Why was Kurtz such a savage? Did he go native and become like the primitive Africans around him? Conrad's descriptions of the Africans are not complimentary, and though he certainly was appalled by Belgian colonialism he remained devoted to the British version.

[[RunningHead]]

On the Therapeutic Action of SSRI Medications

Michael Levinson

Michael Levinson

333 East Ontario Street, #3107B
Chicago, IL 60611
E-Mail: mhlevmd@aol.com

Michael Levinson  

February 2, 2003. Richard Gottlieb supports the analyst's “unique form of listening” when working with patients taking psychotropic medications and correctly notes that “SSRIs challenge traditional diagnostic schemes.” What I do not understand, however, are the conclusions that he has reached as a result of his “listening.” His assumption that the effectiveness of SSRIs for such different conditions as OCD, social anxiety, and depression suggests a common mechanism—e.g., aggression—underlying these different pathologies is reductionistic and a throwback to highly disputable metapsychogical notions. Not only has the traditional drive formulation of aggression been of questionable value for clinical psychoanalysis, but it is even more dubious to apply such a concept to contemporary psychopharmacology.

I understand that Gottlieb emphasizes that his is a “psychoanalytic hypothesis; … not psychiatric, electrochemical, or neuroanatomical.” Yet, despite this disclaimer, his proposal must still support the clinical data. Furthermore, when he refers to the DSM diagnostic categories of Major Depression, Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder, and Social Anxiety Disorder, there is the implicit recognition of the importance of attunement to the syndromal nature of certain psychopathologies. Recognizing the ubiquity, consistency, and bottom-line, sine qua non nature of certain clinical presentations (i.e., as syndromes)

Success and Succession

Irwin Marill and Everett Siegel

Everett Siegel

7004 Carmichael Avenue
Bethesda, MD 20817-4612
E-mail: ihmarill@aol.com

7511 Stream Crossing Road
Baltimore, MD 21209
Fax: 410-614-6031
E-mail: esiegel@jhmi.edu

Everett Siegel   Training and Supervising Analyst, Baltimore-Washington Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, George Washington University School of Medicine; Teaching Faculty, Walter Reed Army Medical Center. Everett R. Siegel, Assistant Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences and Director of Student Mental Health, Johns Hopkins School of Medicine.

The etymology of the word success and its relationship to succession is elucidated. Linkage is made to the consequences of unconscious “legitimate” and “illicit” fulfillments in life. This is explored in the writings of Freud and others. Emphasis is placed on guilt, a major factor blocking the attainment of success. This guilt derives from unconscious parricide, which permeates and contaminates themes of succession. How early objects, particularly oedipal ones, are “killed off” in the process of gaining autonomy and achievement without literally invoking unconscious murder is explored. The retaliatory aspects of failure are also addressed. Illustrations employing case material are used to highlight these theoretical constructs.

[[RunningHead]]

Continued Dialogues in Search of New Knowledge

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser  

Our regularly appearing research sections are designed especially to acquaint our readers with new and ongoing investigations relevant to clinical and conceptual questions within psychoanalysis. In this second of the 2004 sections, we continue to present work intended to provoke lively dialogue among clinicians, scholars, scientists, and educators. The diverse topics covered in past and present contributions include conceptualizing change and outcome in child psychoanalysis; challenges to systematically studying processes in ongoing psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy; the clearer delineation of changes in psychoanalytic education and practice; and the interface between attachment perspectives and other developmental theories, including psychoanalysis.

To present these diverse themes we have used varied formats. Blatt and Shohar's richly conceptual and empirical paper, “Psychoanalysis: With Whom, for What, and How: An Empirical Investigation of Differences between Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy” (JAPA 52/2) focused on an enduring question in psychoanalytic and psychotherapy research, the implications of patients' individual differences (beyond diagnostic ones) for psychotherapy process and outcome. Collections of papers, poster abstracts, exchanges of letters, and discussions around a target paper are other formats we use to foster an understanding of how research can add to psychoanalytic knowledge. In the current intellectual climate, such a deepened understanding of psychoanalytic constructs and clinical experience must often draw on “neighboring disciplines” (Holzman and Aronson 1992) to further clarify psychoanalytic phenomena through patient and analyst reports, as well as through observations of psychoanalytic processes though the observation of analyst-patient interactions. Besides the “enduring dialectic between body and mind” (Mayes 2003, p. 745)

[[RunningHead]]

Assessing Progress in Analysis Interminable

Lawrence Josephs , Eric Anderson , Amy Bernard , Kristine Fatzer and Jodi Streich

Jodi Streich

Garden City, Long Island
NY 11530
E-mail: josephs@adelphi.edu

Jodi Streich  

A case study is provided of a schizoid patient in her mid-sixties who in a lengthy analysis had made significant clinical improvement. The treating analyst's impression of clinical improvement was independently verified through systematic analysis of transcripts of audiotapes of thirty-six sessions over a four-year period of treatment. The patient showed significant improvement on measures of character pathology, object relations, mentalization, and superego anxiety. The results suggest that some patients with entrenched character pathology who seem to be in analysis interminable may still make clinically significant improvement. It is suggested that the scientific status of psychoanalysis would be greatly enhanced if the anecdotal evidence generated in private practice were supported by independent verification. The current study illustrates one methodology, “case study plus,” for providing independent verification while retaining the richness of the traditional case study.

James Barron

James Barron

26 Welland Road
Brookline, MA 02445
E-mail: jwbarron@verizon.net

James Barron   Faculty, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East; Instructor, Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

Arthur Schnitzler (1862-1931) was an inveterate diarist and a prolific novelist, short story writer, and playwright in fin-de-siècle Vienna. He was also an underappreciated figure in the history of psychoanalysis. Born into the Jewish upper middle class, he developed an early interest in creative writing, but obediently followed in the footsteps of his physician father, a prominent laryngologist and university professor. What Schnitzler found most interesting about his medical training was psychiatry. He wrote a review of Freud's translation of Charcot's work (Jones 1957, p. 84) and studied hypnosis as a clinical technique with Theodor Meynert, who was also Freud's teacher. Nonetheless, Schnitzler's attachment to medicine was never as strong as his passionate interest in the arts.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A New Clinical Measure of Overdependence, Detachment, and Healthy Dependency

The Relationship Profile Test

Robert Bornstein and Mary Languirand

Mary Languirand

Box 407
Gettysburg, PA 17325
E-mail: bbornste@gettysburg.edu

Mary Languirand  

Successful psychoanalytic treatment should be accompanied by shifts from overdependence and detachment to healthy dependency. This last is seen in flexible, situation-appropriate seeking of help and support (Bornstein and Languirand 2003). Until recently, however, no psychometrically sound instruments have been available to assess this shift in the clinical setting. The Relationship Profile Test (RPT)—a thirtyitem self-report measure of these three constructs—was developed to fill this gap. Construct validity evidence for the RPT is promising: RPT scores show excellent 85-week retest reliability and predictable associations with scores on measures of related traits (Bornstein et al. 2003). This study examined relations between RPT scores and measures of attachment style, identity, relatedness, and affect in a mixed-sex sample of ninety young adults.

Method

Two measures were administered in addition to the RPT: (1) the Adult Attachment Scale, or AAS (Collins and Read 1990); and (2) the Inventory of Altered Self-Capacities, or IASC (Briere 1998), a widely used measure of identity, relatedness, and affect regulation.

Results

On the AAS, overdependence was associated with insecure attachment, detachment was associated with distrust and discomfort with closeness, and healthy dependency was associated with security and an absence of abandonment fears. On the IASC, overdependence was associated with identity impairment and susceptibility to external influence, whereas detachment was associated with difficulty regulating negative affect. Healthy dependency was associated with optimism, self-confidence, and stable identity.

Discussion

The present findings—along with those of Bornstein et al. (2003)— provide strong evidence supporting the construct validity of the RPT.

[[RunningHead]]

FAILURES IN PSYCHOANALYTIC TREATMENT. Edited by Joseph Reppen and Martin Schulman. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 2003, 365 pp., $39.95.

James Hansell

James Hansell

425 East Washington Street
Suite 202-S
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: jhansell@umich.edu

James Hansell   faculty, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Adjunct Associate Professor of Psychology, University of Michigan.

An important tension animates Failures in Psychoanalytic Treatment, a timely collection of essays edited by Joseph Reppen and Martin Schulman. This tension is evident in the title: note the plural—failures— rather than the singular, failure. Roughly half of the thirteen essays are about failures—particular cases that went or ended badly. The other chapters deal more broadly with the concept of failure in psychoanalysis, a subject about which I will say more. In addition, the editors offer a brief introduction in which they concisely survey the history and variety of psychoanalytic definitions of clinical failure (and success), as well as reviewing major ideas about the common causes of failure. This summary is particularly useful in light of the surprising fact that most of the contributors focus on the causes of failure but say little about how they define it.

The authors of the “failures” essays (including Wallerstein, Tabin, and Paniagua, among others) reflect on what went wrong in their cases, and provide readers plenty of material to come to their own conclusions about how and why the treatments failed. Most of these cases ended up in what could be thought of as sadomasochistic stalemates. (Interestingly, these entanglements frequently focused on analytic frame issues such as fees and schedules.) It is tempting to respond to these cases, as the contributors note that we often tend to do, as a supervisor might. In other words, there is a pull to look for mistakes by the analyst that could account for the outcome. Many of the authors retrospectively supervise themselves this way in their discussions. And, indeed, in virtually every case, the failure could rather easily be explained either by countertransference blind spots or by “unanalyzable” characteristics of the patient.

Such a reading is legitimate, I think, but limited.

1273

[[RunningHead]]

Does Mentalizating Capacity Mediate the Relationship Between Developmental Adversity and Adult Social Functioning?

Helen Stein , Peter Fonagy , Kristine Wheat , Michelle Kipp and Andrew Gerber

Andrew Gerber

135 Central Park West, Suite 1N
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: hbs4@columbia.edu

Andrew Gerber  

Mentalizing is the ability to interpret the actions of self and others on the basis of intentional mental states such as desires, needs, feelings, beliefs, and reasons. It has been demonstrated that developmental adversity can impair the capacity to mentalize (Fonagy et al. 1996) and that for certain individuals the capacity to mentalize may serve

1217

a protective function in the face of trauma (Fonagy et al. 1994). We hypothesized a link between developmental adversity and adult social functioning, where diminished capacity to mentalize is the mediator through which developmental adversity leads to impairment in adult social functioning, particularly in the context of attachment relationships.

Participants

Data were gathered from three samples: (1) 77 men and women, 18 to 37 years of age, formerly treated in a preschool day treatment program for childhood psychopathology, who participated in a followup study; (2) a control group of 48 men and women, matched by age, socioeconomic status, and gender; and (3) a comparison group of 25 referred psychiatric inpatients, ages 19 to 55. Overall, the total sample of 143 participants was 54% male, 46% female, 85% Caucasian, 15% nonwhite, and of low to moderate socioeconomic status.

Measures

All subjects participated in three interviews with established reliability and validity.

Developmental adversity was assessed with Childhood Experiences of Care and Abuse, or CECA (Bifulco et al. 1994), an investigatorbased retrospective interview that measures adversity at home, at school, and in the community from birth to age 18, including antipathy (parental hostility and/or rejection), psychological and physical neglect, discord and violence in the household, and physical, sexual, and psychological abuse. Retrospective information about abuse and neglect obtained from the CECAs of the first sample was checked against detailed childhood records (preschool medical charts, child protective services records). Using principal components analysis of CECA variables across childhood and adolescence, we identified three components: childhood trauma (0-11 years), including all CECA variables listed above; adolescent abuse (12-18), including antipathy, physical and psychological abuse, and psychological neglect; and adolescent neglect (12-18), including family discord and violence, physical and psychological neglect, and sexual abuse.

[[RunningHead]]

Medication Management During Psychoanalysis

Naomi Gwynn and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

350 Central Park West, # 1A
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: ngwynn@aol.com

Steven Roose  

The use of psychotropic medication represents the most prevalent and dramatic change in psychoanalytic technique in the last thirty years. When the psychoanalyst prescribes medication, he or she assumes

1243

a second role and the two functions, psychoanalyst and psychopharmacologist, employ different theoretical frameworks, clinical data, and technical procedures. For example, the appropriate use of psychotropic medication relies on a phenomenologically based diagnostic system and a more traditional doctor-patient relationship.This is obviously different from psychoanalytic metapsychology and technique. Most of the literature has focused on the impact of prescribing medication on the function of the analyst; little has been written about the impact of the analytic situation on the practice of psychopharmacology.

This study reviews the medication management during 77 candidate analytic cases begun at the Columbia University Center for Psycho-analytic Training and Research between 1994 and 1999. At the beginning of analysis, 52% of analytic cases (40/77) had a current Axis I mood or anxiety disorder; 39% (30/77) were on medication at some point during analysis.

Narrative summaries were created for all of the 77 cases from documentation in patients' charts of affective symptoms, Axis I diagnosis, and medication treatment. The narrative summaries illustrate the basis for the candidates' decisions about medication treatment. These narrative summaries suggest that pharmacological decisions during analysis were often based on inadequate or inappropriate data, or on countertransference, or were unduly influenced by theoretical struggles or conflicts between supervisor and candidate.

Overall, this study suggests that medication management during psychoanalysis is often not optimal.

1244
