[[RunningHead]]

Analytic Preparation

The Creation of an Analytic Climate with Patients not Yet in Analysis

Arnold Wilson

Arnold Wilson

80 University Place, #4A
New York, NY 10011
E-mail: dr.arnoldwilson@verizon.net

Arnold Wilson   Faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; faculty, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR).

The art and science of beginning an analysis has a life of its own and can be considered in many ways quite apart from its later stages. An incremental path forward is smoothed for patients who are not yet prepared to analyze, and builds eventually into something readily recognizable as an analysis. The term analytic preparation refers to this set of processes. Early on, the analyst is concerned less with facilitating an early replica of an idealized analysis than with facilitating the mutual adaptation of patient and analyst as they begin to negotiate a “thought community.” Since analytic preparation is not an entity, it does not neatly overlap in real time with the opening phase as usually described, does not have a discrete beginning or end, and does not abruptly shift midstream into analysis proper. Some relations between analytic preparation, analytic interaction, and the interpretation of transference are examined.

At its January 2003 meetings, the American Psychoanalytic Association held the first of what will be annual peer-reviewed poster sessions highlighting the many research studies being conducted by analytic investigators here and abroad. The twenty-one posters that were presented—eighteen of which are represented here by abstracts—reflect a new emphasis in our field on empirical validation and a rich diversity of research topics. As the poster sessions are designed to generate interest and discussion around these endeavors, readers are encouraged to e-mail or otherwise contact the posters' authors.

—Stuart T. Hauser

Conducting Analysis after September 11: Implications for Psychoanalytic Technique

The 9/11/2001 terrorist attack on the World Trade Center profoundly affected the population of New York City, including analysts and analysands. The authors sought to investigate how this affected the psychoanalytic technique of a group of psychoanalysts practicing in the city in the weeks following 9/11.

Method

A questionnaire was developed that asked analysts about their psychoanalytic technique with adult analysands in the days and weeks following 9/11.

Theodore Jacobs introduced the panel saying that the relationship between analytic treatment aims and the goal of positively affecting patients' lives is not clear-cut. Some patients appear engaged in analysis but remain static. They leave discontented, despite having attained “the oft-cited goal of understanding how their minds work.” Others fall short of analytic goals, do not make internal rearrangements, but alter their lives significantly. Such patients may change due to nonanalytic factors, though they believe analytic work caused their improvement. Substantial analytic work can be done without affecting the patient's life, while in other instances major life goals are achieved following meager analytic change. We emphasize insight or structural change, and believe these dynamic changes result in more adaptive compromise formations. This formulation “says nothing about the patient's life.” But in reporting cases we stress “life gains, not new intrapsychic relationships,” though we believe one follows the other. It is said that “we should not be overly concerned with our patients' lives.” Analysts must adhere to analytic goals, and the patient is responsible for using what analysis provides. Thus, “life goals should not enter the mind of the analyst,” lest therapeutic zeal or overinvestment in the patient's problems “muck up the treatment.” Yet we are not content if analytic work yields few life changes. Jacobs suggested that “many of the changes in technique in the direction of more active engagement with patients have been proposed by colleagues who

Leon Wurmser

Leon Wurmser

904 Crestwick Road
Towson, MD 21286

Leon Wurmser   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Freudian Society; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of West Virginia.

During much of its development, psychoanalysis has given scant attention to the affect of shame. The superego was mostly conceived around the questions of an unconscious sense of guilt, of self-punishment, and, perhaps most important, of moral masochism, all very much within the structural theory and the unquestioned centering of psychoanalysis on inner conflict. Then there occurred, about twenty years ago, an almost complete reversal. A number of works have appeared since then that deal very centrally with the shame experience, in examinations often strongly slanted toward interpersonal and social contexts and tied in with early development. The issues of superego, inner conflict, and moral masochism (or masochism altogether) faded more and more into the background. More generally speaking, the pendulum of historical judgment has been swinging farther and farther from the intrapsychic dimension with its conflict and insight orientation and toward the interpersonal/intersubjective dimension with its focus on corrective experience and internalization. Many today eschew the structural and dynamic theory altogether and want to reduce the issues covered by the superego concept of yore by adopting the concepts of values or self-regulating functions and by considering the affects of shame and guilt apart from issues of conflict. There is much merit in this new interest, especially in the serious interest paid to psychic phenomenology in general, and to the study of the affect of shame in particular.

And yet the old alchemistic wisdom and Niels Bohr's motto Contraria sunt complementa (opposites complement each other) (Holton 1973) seems particularly suitable for the progress of understanding in psychoanalysis. We would best understand them as part of a dialectic. Titus, Smith, and Nolan (1986) explain: “The dialectical process is a dialogue between opposing positions.… the dialectic is the development of thought through an interplay of ideas” (p. 10). Hoffman (1994) uses Ogden's more specific definition: “A dialectic is a process in which each of two opposing concepts creates, informs, preserves, and negates the other, each standing in a dynamic (ever changing) relationship with the other” (p. 195). “Psychoanalytic ideas are rooted in a belief in the dialectic,” says Kantrowitz (2001, p. 404).

E-mail: jalundin72@hotmail.com

Sherwood Waldron  

Although there is a great deal of anecdotal evidence supporting the effectiveness of psychoanalysis in changing people's lives for the better, the lack of compelling and rigorous quantitative research demonstrating this impact, as well as the absence of systematic demon- stration of effects of psychoanalytic approaches used in leading to such benefits, has contributed to its underutilization. Moreover, the field has seen fragmentation in terms of both the theory and the technique of psychoanalysis.

One step toward overcoming some of the obstacles hampering research into the process and outcome of psychoanalysis is the development of instruments and methods that can reliably and accurately capture and operationalize the complexities involved. Research into the effectiveness of psychoanalysis has gained momentum in recent years. Yet a great deal of work remains to be done to address a spectrum of questions about process and outcome that both interest and plague our field.

The purpose of this study was to contribute to the development and application of two recently developed measures of “psychological structure,” the Scales of Psychological Capacities (SPC) (DeWitt, Milbrath, and Wallerstein 1991) and the Shedler-Westen Assessment Procedure 200 (SWAP-200) (Shedler and Westen 1998). These two instruments were designed to address methodological problems inherent in psychoanalytic research and also to further efforts to demonstrate the effectiveness of these approaches (Wallerstein 1988; Shedler and Westen 1998). Our study assessed interrater reliability in a new context, specifically in terms of ratings of psychoanalytic process from audiotapes and transcripts. The study also examined change, as reflected in these instruments applied to three psychoanalyses.

Treating Attachment Disorders

From Theory to Therapy. By Karl Heinz Brisch. Translated by Kenneth Kronenberg. New York: Guilford Press, 2002, xxvi + 294 pp., $40.00..

Sara Tucker

2701 Fairfax Road
Cleveland Heights, OH 44118

Sara Tucker   Training and Supervising Analyst, Cleveland Psychoanalytic Center; Assistant Clinical Professor, Case School of Medicine.

In Treating Attachment Disorders: From Theory to Therapy, Karl Heinz Brisch proposes that there can be clinical applications of attachment theory. A child, adolescent, and adult psychiatrist, neurologist, and training psychoanalyst in Munich, Brisch is a gifted clinician who attempts to bridge the gap between the laboratory and the consulting room.

In the first two sections of the book, devoted to a history of attachment theory and its basic concepts starting with Bowlby's pioneering work in the 1960s, and then an overview of attachment disorders, Brisch prepares the reader for the core of the book, his own contributions to the field.

While acknowledging that he cannot adequately cover the current state of empirical attachment research, Brisch does provide a useful review of the literature of attachment research arranged chronologically. In this section he also provides background for the controversies attachment theory stirred within psychoanalysis. He highlights the early longitudinal studies of Mary Ainsworth and her team of developmental psychologists, the test-like standard procedure, the Strange Situation, which she developed to study children's attachment and separation behaviors in a laboratory setting. He goes on to describe and illustrate research tools such as the Adult Attachment Interview and the Separation Anxiety Test.

Brisch focuses on the mother's contribution to the quality of the attachment that over time the infant develops to her. While acknowledging the impact on attachment of the infant's genetic endowment, as well as of life-disruptive events, he does give primary responsibility to the role of mothering. In particular, he discusses “sensitive behavior” by an attachment figure as a requirement for the development of secure attachment.

Kenneth Eisold   President, International Society for the Psychoanalytic Study of Organizations; Supervisor and Fellow, William Alanson White Institute.

Psychoanalysis is not Ralph Stacey's subject. This book is about “social” behavior, as seen primarily in groups and organizations. But what is important here and “radical”—and relevant to psychoanalysts—is that he sees no distinction between the individual and the social. His key idea is that in human experience there are no objects, only

939

processes: this is “an action theory that makes no appeal to notions of inside or outside. Mind is not regarded as an internal world inside a person and social is not regarded as a system, field, matrix, or third, outside a person. The theory of complex responsive processes … is a theory of experience understood as direct interaction between bodies” (p. 17).

Psychoanalysis, though not Stacey's focus, has an important dual relevance to his argument. On the one hand, it is an unrivaled source of observation about the complex layering of human behavior. It cannot be ignored, and Stacey devotes considerable space to its historical and contemporary developments. On the other, it is a theoretical bastion of individualism. To the extent it is committed to the notion of discrete and autonomous selves, relying on the notion of an “inner world,” a bounded realm subject to its own laws, it represents a challenge to be overcome. His point is that the clinical phenomena of psychoanalysis are distorted without the context of the social. It is not that the “social” is more important than the personal or individual, or more real. It is that the distinction is false, an artificial construction. For all our talk about “paradigm shifts,” this is “radical” and new.

Professor of Management and Director of the Complexity and Management Centre at the University of Hertfordshire's Business School, Stacey is also a member of the Institute for Group Analysis in London. He teaches, he manages a university institute, he edits a book series, and he runs therapy groups in the national health service. This is an odd mix but, I think, a carefully chosen one, demonstrating not only his view of what is important about our collective human experience but also where the leverage for change can be found. He works with the managements of organizations and is a group therapist; he does not work with individuals, and it should become more apparent in my discussion below why that is so. This book is aimed at group therapists, particularly his colleagues at the Institute for Group Analysis. The intellectual figure most important in this context is S. H. Foulkes, who was originally trained as a psychoanalyst and who retained a lifelong link to psychoanalytic thought. For Stacey, Foulkes is the original link between psychoanalysis and group work. Here he offers a more radical reworking of that link.

Irwin Hirsch  

The Interpersonal Theory of Psychiatry presents Harry Stack Sullivan's last complete statement of his conceptions about normal and troubled human development. Like most of the volumes containing his work, it was not written by him for publication, but rather was compiled post-humously from lectures and lecture notes, and edited into coherent book form. Most of what is in this volume is taken from his 1946-1947 lecture notes for the Washington School of Psychiatry, Sullivan's primary base (he died in 1949). Though his editors did an excellent job in translation from verbal lecture to printed word, the writing is nonetheless more colloquial than most professional writing, and indeed often reads more like spoken than written text. Sullivan's wry, sarcastic humor is sprinkled throughout, though for some this translation from spoken to written text makes for laborious reading. Sullivan is far too pragmatic to be highly abstract and intellectualized, as so many analytic writers are, but he can be wordy and his idiosyncratic personality can leave readers less than fully engaged. Aside from factors related to the politics of psychoanalysis, his written works have not had as wide an exposure as they might have were he more of a pleasure to read. Given his profound significance in today's psychoanalysis, his work is in general less carefully read than one might expect, even by those identified as within the contemporary interpersonal or relational analytic traditions.

This particular volume, perhaps his most important, represents Sullivan's explication of the development of personality from early infancy through adolescence to early adulthood. I believe it is the first developmental text in psychoanalysis that emphasizes the essence of human development as a function of social/interpersonal interaction.

For anyone interested in the trajectory of psychoanalysis since Freud's earliest formulations, Elizabeth Lunbeck and Bennett Simon present a fascinating, if difficult, opportunity to follow one of the first efforts to provide psychoanalytic treatment in America. Through the session-by-session process notes of Louville Eugene Emerson, one of Freud's earliest followers in America and a self-educated psychoanalyst, the authors present his treatment of Rachel C, a twenty-twoyear-old hysteric. Central also are the commentaries through which the authors situate the case historically, while they enumerate the many difficulties both analyst and patient encountered in the treatment. And there were many, starting with the very infant state of psychoanalysis and hostile climate of the times. This included not only Emerson's fellow psychiatrists at Boston Psychopathic Hospital, to which he had been appointed in 1912, but its director, E. E. Southard, as well.

Emerson was thirty-nine at the time, having come to Freud and psychoanalysis via his studies in philosophy under William James and Josiah Royce at Harvard, where he had earned a Ph.D. in experimental psychology. Afterward he taught briefly at the University of Michigan but, finding teaching limiting and already versed in psychoanalytic literature, he began to study with James Putnam and Morton Prince, leaders of the “so-called Boston school of psychotherapy,” founded in 1911. In time Emerson and Putnam would become friends and colleagues, with Emerson the latter's designated heir.

[[RunningHead]]

Letter

Art and “Regression in the Service of the Ego”

James Hamilton

James Hamilton

58 La Paz Loop
Santa Fe, NM 87508
E-mail: jabfineman@aol.com

James Hamilton  

September 3, 2003. With regard to Laurie Wilson's retrospective review of Ernst Kris's Psychoanalytic Explorations in Art (JAPA 50/4), it should be noted that Phyllis Greenacre, William Niederland, and Philip Weissman can stand with Anton Ehrenzweig and Pinchas Noy among those who have questioned or been critical of Kris's model of “;regression in the service of the ego” as a theoretical framework for the psychoanalytic understanding of the creative process.

Greenacre (1962) did “not see the artist ‘regressing in the service of the ego’ quite as fully as Kris described but would say rather that he has readily at hand and actively available elements of experience which in more ordinarily balanced people could not be reached except by regression” (p. 132). Essentially, this is an extension of her view that the primary process is far more readily accessible in creative individuals.

“Instead of speaking of ‘regression in the service of the ego,’” Niederland (1967) stated, “I am rather inclined to view the situation in connection with the total ego organization which, in the persons under scrutiny, showed both ‘regressive-prone’; and ‘progressive prone’ features in constant interaction under the influence of unalterable body restorative stimuli.… In exploring the lives and ills of creative personalities, one becomes increasingly aware of the great amount of energy available to them. Their energies appear to converge on the creative process and seem to be regression, not so much in the service of the ego as in that of ego restoration, sometimes of ego survival (pp. 20-21).

Weissman (1967) advocated that Kris's concept “be replaced by more detailed statements on the specific ego functions. A more serious problem confronts us in the evaluation of the state of the ego and its function in the creative process. It is a presumptive assessment to state that a controlled regression in the service of the ego takes place during inspiration when we have not as yet specified which specific function or what specific parts might be regressively involved” (p.37).

Stephen Rosenblum   Supervising and Training Analyst, Washington Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, George Washington University Medical School.

It is standard practice to confer the title of training and supervising analyst without requiring proof of competence as a supervisor. The

1275

implicit assumption is that if one knows how to analyze, one knows also how to supervise. That assumption is worth challenging. In their effort to contribute to an independent theory of supervision, the editors of Supervising and Being Supervised: A Practice in Search of a Theory make challenging that assumption one of the themes of their book. The issue is most clearly articulated by the author of the chapter “Problems and Ethical Issues in Supervision.” He notes that supervision requires skills in addition to those needed to be an analyst. However, while we would consider it unethical to practice analysis without formal training, we do not consider it unethical to practice supervision without having learned those additional skills.

The idea for the book emerged from a series of supervision workshops organized by the editors. A number of participants were then invited to contribute chapters to the book, which is organized into four sections: (1) The Supervisory Relationship, (2) Applications and Settings for Supervision, (3) Problems in Practice, and (4) Evolving a Theory of Supervision. The editors provide an introduction and a final overview as a last chapter. A wide range of interesting topics are covered, in addition to the differentiation of supervision from analysis.

The effort to underscore the distinction between supervision and analysis is in part a consequence of the authors' background. The three editors and thirteen other contributors are Jungian-trained analysts from England. Historically, analysis and supervision were not differentiated in Jungian training until the 1960s. Jung was a strong advocate of a personal analysis for all analysts, convinced that the analyst's unconscious elements were the most important factors in his or her work. Extrapolating from Jung, the contributors to this book take the position that the supervisee's unconscious material is the essential work for the supervisor. The editors, in fact, worry that this focus on the supervisee's unconscious is diluted by newer concepts of countertransference that encompass the totality of the analyst's responses to the patient. It is my impression that the supervisory focus for these authors comes perilously close to the analytic focus, forcing them to emphasize the distinctions.

[[RunningHead]]

The Role of the Analyst in the Analytic Cure During Times of Chronic Crises

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan

2 Mohaliver Street
Rehovot, ISRAEL
Fax: 972-8-947-2783
E-mail: ilanyk@yahoo.com

Ilany Kogan   Traiining Analyst, Israel Psychoanalytic Society.

The role of the analyst in psychoanalytic treatment during periods of chronic crises is illustrated with material from two case studies. The first clinical vignette shows an analyst able to stay with fears evoked in the patient by the traumatic external reality, even as the analyst tried to explore with the patient an inner universe that handled this reality in unique ways. The second case study focuses on how the analyst's countertransference during this period of chronic crises, which she was experiencing along with the patient, made it difficult for her to contain the patient's fears and anxieties, because of the threat to her own existence, as well as to her identity as an analyst. In this second case the analyst, out of denial of the external situation, focused blindly on the patient's internal reality in order to counteract her own sense of passivity and helplessness in the confrontation with death and destruction. She clung to “classical” analysis by trying to analyze the patient's defenses, work them through, etc., thus making so-called analytic interpretations rather than staying with the patient's fear, as well as her own, and helping the patient more directly. A turning point came with the birth of the analyst's granddaughter; fear for the new arrival's safety made the analyst sharply aware that it is impossible to ignore external reality, that it must be given a place both in everyday life and in analysis. This awareness enabled the analyst to contain the patients' fears, which helped him feel more supported and facilitated change.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impact of Graduation from Psychoanalytic Training

Sabrina Cherry , Deborah Cabaniss , Nicholas Forand and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

585 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-721-7925
E-mail: sc26@columbia.edu

Steven Roose   Sabrina Cherry, Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons; faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research. Deborah L. Cabaniss, Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research. Nicholas Forand, Ph.D. candidate, American University. Steven P. Roose, Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons; Chair, Research Committee, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

To examine candidates' experience of graduation from psychoanalytic training, 1997-2001 graduates of the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research were sent a confidential questionnaire about their first year after analytic training. Of this group, 72 percent (23/32) returned the survey. Questions focused on the impact of graduation on time availability, net income, professional advancement, and sense of personal and professional autonomy. Graduates from analytic training were found to have more income in their first postgraduate year, a mean increase of $30,000, and more available time, a mean increase of sixteen hours. Increased earnings came primarily from seeing more patients during the time made available with the end of classes. In addition, graduates did not terminate their control cases or stop supervision. Graduates most valued their sense of professional accomplishment and ability to spend more time with their families. Although graduates also experienced relief from evaluation pressure, they did not rank this high in importance. For candidates, graduation profoundly impacts the structure of professional and personal life, but does not mean an end to learning.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impact of Graduation from Analytic Training

Sabrina Cherry , Deborah Cabaniss , Nicholas Forand and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

585 West End Avenue
Suite 1E
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: sc26@columbia.edu

Steven Roose  

While many aspects of psychoanalytic training have been critiqued and researched, strikingly little has been written about the criteria for graduation. Whether graduation marks the certification of competency or a transition to a new phase of analytic education is still debated among faculty. It is generally accepted, however, that developing proficiency as an analyst requires time beyond formal training. Candidates' experience of graduation and its impact on personal and professional life have never been studied.

This study was designed to examine the experience of graduation, particularly in the first year after analytic training. We sent graduates of the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research from 1997 through 2001 an anonymous questionnaire about their first year after analytic training. Questions focused on the impact of graduation on time availability, net income, professional advancement, and sense of personal and professional autonomy.

Results

Seventy-two percent (23/32) returned the survey. We found that in the first postgraduate year graduates had more income, a mean net increase of $30,000 per year, and more available time, a mean net increase of 16.5 hours per week. The majority of the total time gained resulted from the ending of classes, together with related travel and preparation (69%, 11.5/16.5 hours). Much less time was gained because of completing control cases (4%, 0.6/16.5 hours) or stopping supervision (12%, 2.0/16.5 hours). The 61% of our sample who ended their training analysis in the first postgraduate year also gained further time (15%, 2.5/16.5 hours). In addition, there were financial benefits, which resulted primarily from seeing more patients during the time made available with the end of classes. Graduates most valued their sense of professional accomplishment and ability to spend more time with their families. Although graduates also experienced relief from evaluation pressure, they did not rank it high in importance compared to other changes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Defense Mechanisms and Violence toward Partners and Strangers

Defense Mechanism Manual and Defense Mechanisms Rating Scale

Rosemary Cogan , John Pocerelli , Ray Kamoo and Susan Leitman

Susan Leitman

18th Street and Boston Avenue
Lubbock, TX 79409-2051
E-mail: rcogan@ttacs.ttu.edu

Boomfield Hills, MI

Susan Leitman  

Violence is common in late adolescence and among college students. Late adolescence, an important time in intrapsychic development, is characterized by a shift to the use of more mature defense mechanisms (Cramer 1991). Immature defense mechanisms may not be sufficient to control aggression. We hypothesized that people with more mature defense mechanisms would use less violent means of conflict resolution with both partners and strangers.

Method

Thirty-eight undergraduate college student men (average age 19.5 years) in beginning psychology classes made up stories about each of six cards (1, 2, 4, 6BM, 7BM, and 13MF) from the Thematic Apperception Test. The students also described six important interactions with other people, following the Relationship Anecdotes Paradigms (RAP) interview procedure (Luborsky and Crits-Christoph 1998). Each student then completed a measure of interpersonal problem solving and violence, the Conflict Tactics Scale (Straus 1979).

TAT stories and RAP interview transcripts were scored for defenses. The Defense Mechanisms Manual, or DMM (Cramer 1991), was used to score TAT responses. The DMM assesses three defense mechanisms: denial (least mature), projection (intermediate), and identification (most mature). The Defense Mechanism Rating Scale, or DMRS (Perry 1990), was used to score RAP responses. The DMRS assesses seven levels of defenses: mature, obsessional, other neurotic, minor image-distorting, disavowal, major image-distorting, and acting out. The TAT and RAP data were reliably scored by two psychoanalytic clinicians.

Results

Although participants produced an equal number of TAT stories and RAP episodes, there were fewer DMM TAT defense scores (13.4 per protocol) than DMRS defense scores (5.3 per protocol), t(37) = 9.81, p < .001. However, there were more words for the RAP narratives (1,683) than for the TAT stories (1,097), t(37) = 4.63, p < .001.

477

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Shame and Guilt in Male Aggression toward Partners

Cynthia Moore , Erika Dunkelberg , Laura Chivers , Jacqueline O'Berg and Robert Waldinger

Robert Waldinger

1249 Boylston Street 3rd floor
Boston, MA 02115
Fax: 815-377-6637
E-mail: rwaldinger@partners.org

Robert Waldinger  

Physical abuse histories are common among men who engage in physical violence toward their partners (Dutton 2000; Margolin, John, and Foo 1998). Research has begun to focus on how shame and guilt regulate the expression of aggression (Dutton 1999; Tangney et al. 1996). In this study we hypothesized that proneness to shame and guilt would predict men's aggression toward their partners, even after accounting for men's physical abuse histories.

Method

Using community advertisements, we recruited 52 heterosexual, English-speaking couples, married or living together for at least a year. The ethnic makeup of the sample reflected that of the greater Boston area. Couples completed a series of questionnaires assessing (1) the severity of childhood physical abuse—Childhood Trauma Questionnaire (Bernstein and Fink 1998); (2) guilt and shame proneness— Test of Self Conscious Affect (Tangney, Wagner, and Gramzow 1989; and (3) frequency of current physical aggression toward partners— Revised Conflict Tactics Scale (Straus et al. 1996).

Results and Discussion

Using linear regression analysis to control for men's history of physical abuse, we found that men who were more prone to experience shame were more frequently aggressive toward their partners, whereas proneness to guilt was related to less frequent aggression. These results suggest that further exploration of the role of shame and guilt in domestic violence is warranted. Knowledge of men's proneness to shame and guilt may increase our ability to predict the frequency of their aggressive behavior toward intimate partners.

480

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Therapeutic Action of SSRI Medications

Larry Sandberg

Larry Sandberg

205 East 78th Street, Suite 1A
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: mcsand1293@aol.com

Larry Sandberg  

November 15, 2002. I read with interest Richard Gottlieb's “A Psychoanalytic Hypothesis Concerning the Therapeutic Action of SSRI Medications” and would like to comment on his suggestion that “the essential benefits of these medications derive from their capacity to modify aggression.”

The modification in aggression he describes has been well documented in the clinical and laboratory research literature (Fava 1998; Fuller 1996; Grimes and Milloni 2002). However, I think it is highly unlikely that serotonin reuptake inhibition—which creates a cascade of changes at the precortical and cortical levels—has such a unitary mechanism of action. While “selective” viz-à-viz serotonin reuptake inhibition, serotonin's effect on the brain is hardly specific. Antonio Damasio (1994), while supporting Gottlieb's general impression that enhancing serotonergic function tends to reduce aggression, also notes that “serotonin is one of the main neurotransmitters … whose actions contribute to virtually all aspects of behavior and cognition” (p. 76). Moreover, given the generally accepted importance of aggression in mental functioning among psychoanalysts, the improvement of any individual—by any means—will of necessity result in a modification of aggression in a favorable direction. However, this does not mean it is through a primary modification of aggression that the intervention has its effect. It is far more likely that the commonality Gottlieb is describing derives from the way in which serotonin reuptake inhibition—by virtue of its nonspecificity—can be therapeutic. Thus, many depressives experience a shift in their internal world whereby selfand object representations are less aggressively tinged. This is particularly striking, from the analytic perspective, in patients for whom masochistic character traits have appeared intractable. A study by Coccaro and Kavoussi (1997) specifically studied the SSRI fluoxetine and impulsive aggressive behavior in personality disorders and similarly found that aggression is decreased. The social phobic patient who responds to an SSRI is inevitably more comfortable with aggression, but here it is probably through the essential reduction of anxiety (and the distorting effect of anxiety on cognition) that such patients are able to be more comfortable with their aggression. Finally, I am not aware of any literature that supports the notion that obsessionals who respond to SSRIs do so as a “consequence of modification of the aggression that drives them.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Points on a Compass

Four Views on the Developmental Theories of Margaret Mahler and John Bowlby

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of California-San Diego School of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst and Supervising Child Analyst, San Diego Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Geographic Supervising Child Analyst, Denver Psychoanalytic Institute and Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

Contemporary neurobiological research studies, using technology developed during “the decade of the brain,” reveal that emotional interactions during the early years of life have a definitive role in determining brain morphology. These studies confirm the observations of earlier analysts like August Aichhorn, D. W. Winnicott, John Bowlby, René Spitz, Anna Freud, and Margaret Mahler about the ways early experiences with caregivers influence later psychic functioning. This confirmation comes at a time when psychoanalysts are more accepting of new ideas, have greater tolerance for ambiguity and plurality, and more fully recognize the complexities of human development. Analysts currently seem more open to considering ways in which nonlinear dynamic systems theory, which addresses the complexity and fluidity of the developmental process, may be more relevant to psychoanalysis than either/or polarizations such as drive vs. object relations theories, and one-person versus two-person technical approaches. Many psychoanalysts are also recognizing the value of empirical research and are willing to consider empirical findings. These shifts present greater possibilities for dialogue not only among differing schools of thought within psychoanalysis, but also with neighboring sciences whose focus, methodology, and data are different from our own.

In the year 2000, JAPA (48/4) published a series of papers that began such a dialogue, one between attachment theory and psychoanalysis. That dialogue made clear that further discussion and rapprochement between the theories of Margaret Mahler, contemporary developmental research findings, and John Bowlby were not only necessary but essential. The following four papers, each pursuing a different direction, continue that discussion.

499

[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality

Sexual Orientation and Psychoanalysis: Sexual Science and Clinical Practice. By Richard C. Friedman and Jennifer I. Downey New York: Columbia University Press, 2002, 363 pp., $36.50.

Henry Friedman

Henry Friedman

6 Garden Terrace
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-Mail: friedmanhj@aol.com

Henry Friedman   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Teaching and Supervising Analyst, Massachusetts Institute of Psychoanalysis.

Despite Freud's oft-quoted letter to the concerned mother of a homosexual son (a humane and liberal statement), psychoanalysts who followed him managed to undermine his enlightened position by substituting a view of homosexuality as a pathological outcome of faulty development. Psychoanalysis evolved a position in which homosexual patients were viewed as unanalyzable unless they were able, through the exploration and resolution of unconscious oedipal conflicts, to become heterosexual in their orientation. The insistence in the United States on the pathological nature of homosexuality has faded under the influence of a number of changes in our society, but the association of homosexuality with both oedipal and preoedipal pathology persists both here and in other parts of the world. This book, by two psychoanalysts who have consistently made contributions to the psychoanalytic literature on homosexuality, is an important step toward making psychoanalysis again relevant to the study of sexual orientation. As such, and to ensure the correction of past errors, it should be required reading for analysts everywhere.

Richard Friedman and Jennifer Downey, drawing on years of work, explore here how psychoanalysis became a center for homophobic thinking and the misunderstanding of homosexuality. In their opinion, this misapplication of psychoanalysis resulted from a combination of unquestioned acceptance of classical theory, with its overemphasis on the oedipal determination of adult sexuality, and an authoritarian endorsement of homophobic beliefs.

[[RunningHead]]

WRITING AND MADNESS: LITERATURE/PHILOSOPHY/PSYCHOANALYSIS. By Shoshana Felman. Palo Alto: Stanford University Press, 2003, viii + 294 pp., $55.00 hardcover, $19.95 paperback.

Lois Oppenheim

Lois Oppenheim

181 East 73rd Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-988-9684
E-mail: oppenheimL@mail.montclair.edu

Lois Oppenheim   Distinguished Scholar, Professor of French, and Chair, Department of French, German, and Russian, Montclair State University; candidate, Scholars Program, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

At his death, Freud was confirmed in his status as icon by W. H. Auden, who famously referred to him as “a whole climate of opinion.” Reading Writing and Madness, one has the sense of an author grappling with the challenge posed, however surreptitiously, by the poet. Indeed, originally published in 1978 as La Folie et la chose littéraire, and in English translation some seven years later, the volume seeks to define modern literature (both high and post-) by its relation to psychoanalysis. More precisely, it seeks “literature's constitutive relation to what culture has excluded under the label ‘madness’”; it aims also to uncover the means by which the writer, in reclaiming the “discourse of the madman,” dramatizes “something essential about the relation between literature and power, as well as between literature and knowledge” (p. 2).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Patient factors associated with outcome of psychoanalysis

Sheila Gray and Lee Brauer

Lee Brauer

P.O. Box 40612
Palisades Station
Washington DC 20016-0612
E:mail: shgray@post.harvard.edu

Lee Brauer  

During the course of drafting position papers for the American Psychoanalytic Association, questions frequently arose about factors that affect the duration and outcome of psychoanalysis. In the 1996 Survey of Analytic Practice, all members of the American Psychoanalytic Association were asked to submit information about their three most recently terminated analytic cases. We were able to identify demographic information and DSM-III-R diagnoses for 972 terminated cases submitted by 355 analysts. Outcome was measured by whether the case failed, was interrupted, or was completed and by changes in the GAF (DSM-III-R Axis V) components scored according to a protocol of demonstrated reliability that was provided to all study participants.

Diagnosis

Ninety-six percent of the cases submitted for this study had at least one reported Axis I or Axis II diagnosis. Eighty-two percent had an Axis I diagnosis. Seventy-three percent of patients who had any Axis I disorder also had a comorbid Axis II (Personality) Disorder. Thirteen percent of the study cases had only an Axis II Disorder. Distribution of cases with a reported Axis II diagnosis: Cluster A = 1%; Cluster B = 39%; Cluster C = 30%; Personality Disorder N.O.S. = 30%.

Factors Affecting Completion

Completion rates varied significantly within Axis I. The average completion rate was 60%, but it was only 30% for Social Phobia and 39% for Major Affective Disorder, Recurrent.

The Influence of Prenatal Triadic Capacities on Early Mother-Father-Infant Interaction Patterns

Agnes von Wyl , Kai von Klitzing , Sonja Perren and Dieter Bürgin

Dieter Bürgin

Schaffhauserrheinweg 55
CH-4058 Basel
Switzerland
E-mail: agnes.vonwyl@unibas.ch

Dieter Bürgin  

Both clinical psychoanalysts and developmental psychologists consider the relationships and interactions between mother and child to have an influence on the latter's development. The father, particularly his developing relationship with the child, has largely been neglected. In our work, we have tried to extend the investigation of the dyadic relationship between mother and child to include the father and the family triad. An important thrust of our work has been to examine determinants of the developing triadic relationship across the transition to new parenthood.

On the Therapeutic Action of SSRI Medications

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser  

Richard M. Gottlieb's “A Psychoanalytic Hypothesis Concerning the Therapeutic Action of SSRI Medications” (JAPA50/3, pp. 969-971) has drawn considerable comment on a topic at the interface of psychoanalysis and psychopharmacology. Seldom has such a brief contribution—it is barely two and a half pages long—generated such substantial discussion. Following are five letters addressing the article and a reply by its author.

—Stuart T. Hauser

responseSet.authorMast: Edith M. Cooper,  Lisa A. Mellman,  Anna Kramarsky and  Jill Harkavy Friedman
responseSet.authorCitation: Cooper, E. M., Mellman, L. A., Kramarsky, A. & Friedman, J. H.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Symptoms, Defenses, and the Therapeutic Alliance

Findings after one year of Psychodynamic Psychotherapy

Edith Cooper , Lisa Mellman , Anna Kramarsky and Jill Friedman

Jill Friedman

440 West End Avenue, #1D
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: emc18@columbia.edu

Jill Friedman  

Seventy-three students were referred from a university mental health center to a hospital-based treatment and research clinic for twice-weekly psychodynamic psychotherapy. The first year of treatment was assessed with regard to symptom improvement, development of a therapeutic alliance, and shifts in defense style. The authors hypothesized (1) that symptoms of depression and anxiety would decrease; (2) that

452

use of mature defenses would increase, while use of immature and neurotic defenses would decrease; and (3) that the therapeutic alliance would strengthen over the course of the year. In addition, the relation-ships among symptoms, defenses, and therapeutic alliance were investigated.

Data were collected at three points during the year. At entry into treatment, Axis I and II diagnoses were assessed, as were levels of depression, anxiety, and defense styles. At two months, the early therapeutic alliance was measured. At one year, levels of depression, anxiety, therapeutic alliance, and defense style were reevaluated.

Results

Results indicated that subjects began treatment with high levels of depression and anxiety, in addition to substantial Axis I and II psychopathology. At entry into treatment, those who carried both Axis I and II diagnoses were more depressed, more anxious, and more likely to use immature defenses than those who carried an Axis I diagnosis only or received no diagnosis. This suggests an interaction between Axis I and II that heightens psychopathology.

When assessed at two months, the level of therapeutic alliance was found to be both strong and unrelated to diagnosis, indicating the capacity of those with even substantial DSM-IV symptomatology to form a therapeutic connection. Contrary to expectation, the therapeutic alliance did not become stronger over the year in treatment, perhaps due to the high level at which it started. However, an unexpected relationship between defenses and therapeutic alliance emerged. Those who scored high in use of mature defenses began therapy more cautiously with regard to the therapeutic alliance, but ended the year more strongly allied with the therapist than those who used predominantly immature or neurotic defenses. As expected, there was a significant reduction in symptoms of distress, depression, and anxiety by the end of the first year of treatment. While there was a trend toward decreased use of immature defenses, defenses did not change over the year, suggesting an enduring aspect to defense style that would require a longer treatment to address.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Therapeutic Action of SSRI Medications

Mortimer Ostow

Mortimer Ostow

4421 Douglas Avenue
Bronx, NY 10471
E-mail: mostow1234@aol.com

Mortimer Ostow  

January 15, 2003. Richard Gottlieb is to be congratulated for his attempt to understand the efficacy of SSRI medication in psychoanalytic terms. The editors of JAPA are similarly to be recognized for their willingness to further the dialogue between psychoanalysis and psychopharmacology.

That said, I should like to record a few difficulties I have with Gottlieb's thesis. He proposes that SSRIs are effective for the treatment of depression and some other conditions because, by whatever neurochemical mechanism, they attenuate or remove aggression. His evidence consists of the common clinical observation that when depression, obsessive-compulsive neurosis, or some cases of social anxiety are treated with SSRIs, the aggression disappears as the patient recovers.

The trouble I have with his argument is primarily that the relation between SSRIs and the removal of aggression is not specific.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Factors Associated with Outcome of Psychoanalysis

Intensity, Analyst Status, Analyst Immersion

Sheila Gray and Lee Brauer

Lee Brauer

P.O. Box 40612
Palisades Station
Washington, DC 20016
E-mail: shgray@post.harvard.edu

Lee Brauer  

During the course of drafting position papers for the American Psycho-analytic Association, questions frequently arose about factors that affect the quality and outcome of psychoanalytic treatment. Based on data submitted to the 1996 Survey of Psychoanalytic Practice by 355 analysts for 972 of their three most recently terminated cases, we delineated characteristics of analysts and their practice style supporting or impeding “good” outcomes. Outcomes were measured (1) by whether the case failed, was interrupted, or was completed; and (2) by change in the patient's Global Adaptive Functioning (GAF) score (DSM-III-R Axis V). Analyst variables included (1) certification; (2) institute status; and (3) extent of immersion in analytic practice.

Results

Are four sessions a week better than three? No. Contrary to generally held belief, we found no difference in outcome between treatments conducted at frequencies of three and four sessions a week (Least Squares Difference [LSD] Test, p = .28).

Are five-sessions-a-week analyses better than three-or four-sessions-a-week treatment? Yes. Cases treated at five sessions a week showed a significantly better chance of achieving successful completion (LSD Test: 3 vs. 5 sessions, p = .009; 4 vs. 5 sessions, p = .014).

Are ordinary clinical psychoanalysts certified by the Board on Professional Standards (OCPBs) better than those who are not certified (OCPs)? No. We found no significant difference between the outcomes of these two groups (Pearson's chi-square test showed no significant differences between any outcomes by analyst group).

Do training analysts show better completion-of-analysis rates than ordinary clinical analysts not certified (OCPs) or ordinary clinical analysts with certification (OCPBs)? Yes. Training analysts have significantly better completion rates for cases at all frequencies of treatment (p < .05) and for cases treated five times a week (Pearson chi-square = 28.14, p = .0001, df = 4).

Does the initial mental health of a patient contribute to his or her outcome? Yes.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Theory of Culture

Eros, Loss, and Politics. By Abraham Drassinower. Lanham, MD: Rowman & Littlefield, 2003, xi + 208 pp., $65.00 hardbound, $29.95 paperback.

Robert Paul

Robert Paul

400 Candler Library
550 Asbury Circle
Atlanta, GA 30322
Fax: 404-727-9360
E-mail: rpaul@emory.edu

Robert Paul   Dean of Emory College, Faculty of Arts and Sciences, Charles Howard Candler Professor of Anthropology and Interdisciplinary Studies, Associate Professor in the Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Emory University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute.

This is a thoughtful, closely reasoned, and well-argued book that takes very seriously Freud's status as a theorist of culture and philosopher of life and society. Drassinower's strategy relies on a fine-grained exegesis of some passages in Freudian texts that are less written about in the literature than many others, beginning with the 1915 essay “Thoughts for the Times on War and Death.” His aim to address the question of whether Freud is indeed a “pessimist” on the question of the possibilities for a just and peaceful world, and whether he really meant it when he wrote in the penultimate sentence of Civilization and Its Discontents that “now it is to be expected that the other of the two ‘Heavenly Powers’ [Eros and Death], eternal Eros, will make an effort to assert himself in the struggle with his equally immortal adversary”—an expectation he qualified in the final sentence, asking, “But who can foresee with what success and with what result?” (Freud 1930, p. 145).

273

responseSet.title: Letters: On the Patient's Right to Tell
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters

On the Patient's Right to Tell

Tamara Feldman

Tamara Feldman

120 Curtis Ave
Medford, MA 02155
E-mail: drfeldman@hotmail.com
E-mail: tamara.feldman@tufts.edu

Tamara Feldman  

January 13, 2004. I would like to offer a few thoughts about Rachel Blass's “On the Patient's Right to Tell and the Ethical Reality of Psychoanalysis” (JAPA 51/4). In her paper, Blass examines the ethical limits of free association. She discusses specific circumstances that may require someone in a nonanalytic relationship to refrain from disclosing information for ethical reasons, as when the disclosure is “something that one was told in confidence” or “something that is harmful to another person” (p. 1287). She then explores whether these circumstances apply to patients in an analytic relationship.

Blass's discussion has important implications for training analyses. In a training analysis, both analyst and patient are members of an intimate community rife with opportunities to breach confidences and disclose potentially harmful information about other community members. Unlike other analytic dyads, the participants in a training analysis are members of a small community that fulfills professional, educational, and social functions. Moreover, power is not shared equally among all members but rather is distributed in a hierarchical fashion based on one's role in the analytic institute. Because of these unique features, the patient/analyst-in-training may discuss people whom the analyst not only knows, but with whom the analyst is personally and professionally involved, or over whom the analyst has influence. These may include the analyst's friends, colleagues, supervisees, analysands, and spouse.

Given the shared community of training analyst and patient/analyst-in-training, the patient's disclosures about members of their community may be hurtful to those members, or to the training analyst. For example, should the patient/analyst-in-training discuss experiences in class or in supervision that reflect negatively on the teacher or supervisor? Might this disclosure adversely affect the training analyst's opinion of the analyst being discussed? If the analyst about whom the patient is speaking is a close friend, lover, or spouse of the patient's analyst, could the information be hurtful to the analyst?

responseSet.title: The Sense of Self in Anorexia Nervosa Patients: A Psychoanalytically Informed Method for Studying Self-Representation
responseSet.authorMast: Susan A. Bers,  Sidney J. Blatt and  Ann Dolinsky
responseSet.authorCitation: Bers, S. A., Blatt, S. J. & Dolinsky, A.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Sense of Self in Anorexia Nervosa Patients

A Psychoanalytically Informed Method for Studying Self-Representation

Susan Bers , Sidney Blatt and Ann Dolinsky

Ann Dolinsky

495 Orange Street
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: susan.bers@yale.edu

Ann Dolinsky  

Though writing from divergent perspectives, researchers and clinicians view eating disorders as fundamentally disorders of the self, manifested, for instance, as problems in self-esteem, self-perception, and self-regulation. Other psychiatric patients also have such disorders. The aim of the study, therefore, was to explore a central psychological feature of eating disorders, the sense of self, and to identify aspects unique to hospitalized anorexia nervosa patients so as to distinguish them from other psychiatric patients, as well as from nonpatients.

The sense of self was assessed with open-ended self-descriptions. Seventy-seven females aged 14 to 24 were asked to describe themselves. They met criteria for one of three groups: Anorexia Nervosa Patients (n = 15),Control Patients (n = 15), and Control Nonpatients (n = 47). The self-descriptions were rated on eighteen scales, which fell into four factors and two affective scales. The self-descriptions of the three groups were compared statistically on the six variables.

The two patient groups shared characteristics that significantly differentiated them in two ways from women who were not patients:(1) lower levels of Agency (indicating less investment in chosen areas; a more negative, fragmented self-regard; a relatively unarticulated sense of self; and more despair over defining or becoming the person they wanted to be); and (2) lower levels of Relatedness (indicating more isolation from or dependency on others; feeling rejected or judged by others; and a low interest in issues of relatedness). There were no significant differences among the three groups in Differentiation.What significantly differentiated the anorexia nervosa patients from both the other psychiatric patients and the nonpatients was a heightened Self-reflectivity in their self-descriptions (indicating more self-reflection; a less concrete, more internal focus; more contradictions about the self; and harsher judgments of the self) and more expressions of Depression and Anxiety. While all the psychiatric patients had a negative view of themselves, that of the anorexics occurred in the context of intense self-scrutiny and negative affect.

On Being and Having a Mother. By Erna Furman. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 2001, 242 pp., $ 29.95 paperbac.

Howard Benensohn

Howard Benensohn

4545 Connecticut Avenue, NW #306
Washington, DC 20008
Fax: 301-652-4568
E-mail: Benensohn@earthlink.net

Howard Benensohn   Chair, Education Committee, and Director, Child / Adolescent Analysis Program, Washington Psychoanalytic Institute; Associate Clinical Professor, Georgetown University; Associate Clinical Professor, George Washington University.

Erna Furman was a dedicated child therapist and analyst, a prolific writer whose several books and nearly two hundred articles span half a century. This collection of articles, which, sadly, was to be her last, had a dual purpose. First, in response to requests from “colleagues, friends, and parents,” it was meant, she wrote, “to bring together my various papers on parenting, and especially on mothers and mothering, because some that were published were not readily available and a number never appeared in print.” Second, she said, it addressed “my own need to integrate my thinking and feeling by reaching back to its professional beginning, almost forty years ago, by following in my own footsteps, and assessing how far I have been able to come in my understanding” (p. vii).

625

responseSet.title: The Structured Interview For Personality Organization (Stipo): Reliability and Validity
responseSet.authorMast: Barry L. Stern,  Eve Caligor,  Steven P. Roose and  John F. Clarkin
responseSet.authorCitation: Stern, B. L., Caligor, E., Roose, S. P. & Clarkin, J. F.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Structured Interview For Personality Organization (Stipo)

Reliability and Validity

Barry Stern , Eve Caligor , Steven Roose and John Clarkin

John Clarkin

21 East 40th Street
Penthouse Suite
New York NY 10016
E-mail: blstern@mailshell.com

John Clarkin  

The Structured Interview for Personality Organization (STIPO) is an outgrowth of Kernberg's conceptual model (1984) positing three levels of personality organization: neurotic, borderline, and psychotic. These levels of personality organization derive from an assessment that places particular emphasis on the ego functions of reality testing, sense of identity, and defensive style. The STIPO assesses these dimensions, as well as quality of object relations, coping/rigidity, aggression, and moral functioning, dimensions also thought to be determinative of personality organization.

The STIPO differs from measures typically found in the personality assessment literature by its sampling of psychoanalytically oriented constructs (e.g., defenses, quality of object relations) and its assess- ment of functioning across the spectrum of normal and disordered personality. The STIPO differs from other psychoanalytically informed measures of personality in its clear grounding in a specific theory of personality organization, and its highly structured and standardized format of administration.

The objectives of this preliminary reliability/validity study were to demonstrate the internal consistency of the STIPO subscales, and to test the convergent validity of the STIPO—i.e., correlations between STIPO domains and variables relevant to character pathology and health (e.g., measures of aggression, negative temperament, and indices of Axis II symptoms).

Data for this study were pooled from two ongoing investigations involving the STIPO.

1223

[[RunningHead]]

Why do Women Love Men and Not their Mothers? By Marie-Christine Hamon; translated by Susan Fairfield. New York: Other Press, 2000, 260 pp., $22.00 paperback.

Paula Bernstein

Paula Bernstein

4900 Cherry Creek South Drive
Suite D
Denver, CO 80246-2283
Fax: 303-722-5731
E-mail: wpbernstein@compuserve.com

Paula Bernstein   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Colorado Health Sciences Center; faculty, Denver Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Why Do Women Love Men and Not Their Mothers? reconstructs a fertile period in the history of psychoanalysis, the time between about 1920 and 1933 when Freud's colleagues debated his theories of female development. Rereading Freud's papers and those of his contemporaries, tracing back and forth the borrowings of one paper from another, Marie-Christine Hamon exposes hidden dialogues, contributions integrated in one way or another into Freud's thinking and many ideas relegated to the dustbin of his disapproval. She conveys the power of Freud's influence and the tensions within his circle.

Hamon was curious to know how the clinical observations of Freud's contemporaries aided him in what she sees as his passage from “uncertainty” in the seminal papers written from 1923 to 1925, to “confidence” in 1931 and 1933. As late as 1920, Freud assumed a simple parallel between the oedipal complexes of boys and girls, the little girl naturally desiring her father and hating her mother as her rival in love. By 1925, he had reorganized his theory of the girl's entry into the oedipus complex as a function of her discovery of the “fact” of her castration. Much about the girl's preoedipal attachment to her mother remained obscure to him, though, because his male gender tended to bias the transferences toward him. Several years later, with the publication of the 1931 and 1933 papers on female sexuality, Hamon finds everything formulated earlier about “the girl's phallic activity and her penis envy … reaffirmed, but reordered as a function of the mother”; in order to love men, girls must renounce “a virility that from the outset belongs to them as much as it does to boys,” and, like boys, girls must give up their first love object. As with boys, the shift toward the father occurs only with the recognition of the mother's castration (pp. 1-2).

Hamon reads the papers written by Freud and his associates through the lens of Lacan's formulations. Her assessments of the contributions of so many analysts—notably H. W. van Ophuijsen, Jeanne Lampl—de Groot, August Starcke, Karl Abraham, Helene Deutsch, Melanie Klein, and Ruth Mack Brunswick—are in the service of her own interest in tracing Freud's progress toward a nearly Lacanian understanding of the critical importance of the mother's castration.

[[RunningHead]]

Violence and Subjectivity. Edited by Veena Das, Arthur Kleinman, Mamphela Ramphele, and Pamela Reynolds. Berkeley: University of California Press, 2000, 387 pp., $55.00.

Judith Filc

Judith Filc

71 Sixth Street, 2nd Floor
Pelham, NY 10803
E-mail: judy@erichsound.com

Judith Filc   Translator, International Journal of Psychoanalysis.

Violence and Subjectivity is a compilation of essays by social scientists who have carried out their fieldwork in a broad range of sociocultural milieus. The focus varies considerably, from the portrayal of social violence in the media to the circumcision ritual within the Muslim community in India and its ties to collective violence. What brings these papers together is the authors' vantage point. There is a shared view of the role of violence in the shaping of subjectivity in an era characterized by transnationalization. In the editors' words, “it becomes necessary to consider how subjectivity … is produced through the experience of violence and the manner in which global flows involving images, capital, and people become entangled with local logics in identity formation” (p. 1). The study of the role of violence in the construction of subjectivity allows for analysis of the ways in which “moral processes” and “emotional conditions” intertwine.

Since the book's essays approach this problematic with very different objects of study, requiring the exploration of very different dimensions of social and cultural processes—state-citizen relations, family dynamics, global flows—they encourage readers to develop new ways of conceptualizing the mode of articulation between public and private: the space of politics and the space of intimacy. The editors' definition of subjectivity already places the latter in the interface between social and individual. They claim that subjectivity is “the felt interior experience of the person that includes his or her positions in a field of relational power” (p. 1).

Three dimensions may be identified in these essays that create layers of interpretation, revealing the complexity of the matter at hand: (1) the articulation of global, local, and individual; (2) the effect of violence on individual and collective perceptions and representations; and (3) the inscription in bodies of the violence of domination.

Global, local, individual. In this dimension, the authors analyze the ways in which global, national, and local institutions interact in the production of daily practices and representations. Global institutions comprise multilateral agencies (such as the humanitarian agencies studied by Woodward), the media (as in Kleinman's essay), and the biotechnological industry (explored by Lock)

[[RunningHead]]

Diagnosis of Ovarian Cancer

Regression in Early Memories in the Face of Danger

Rosemary Cogan , Allen Stringer , Jann Aldredge-Clanton and John Porcerelli

John Porcerelli

Texas Tech University
Lubbock, TX 79409-2051
E-mail: r.cogan@ttu.edu

John Porcerelli  

Being diagnosed with a major disease presents a traumatic threat. Ovarian cancer is one of the most deadly gynecological malignancies and leads to a focus on the importance of the social support of family and friends or feelings of isolation and lack of support. To want and need support from and closeness with family and friends in the face of the diagnosis of ovarian cancer seems adaptive, a kind of regression in the face of danger. Like other ego functions, early memories change as a function of current conflicts.

Based on Freud's screen memories paper (1899), Mayman developed an early memories procedure that has been shown to be an excellent tool for assessing current psychological functioning. Mayman (1968) also developed a scale that can be used in clinical or experimental situations for scoring“self-other relationship representations in early memories” (Mayman's libidinal level scale). The scale ranges from oral to latency configurations. We explored differences in the libidinal relationship themes and affect tone in the early memories of 13 women newly diagnosed with ovarian cancer and 13 community women.

1242

[[RunningHead]]

Power

Feeling Strong: The Achievement of Authentic Power. By Ethel S. Person. New York: William Morrow / Harper-Collins, 2002, 432 pp., $25.95.

Frederic Levine

Frederic Levine

7600 Red Road
Suite 108
Miami, FL 33143
Fax: 305-665-9988
E-mail: fredericjl@aol.com

Frederic Levine   Training and Supervising Analyst, Florida Psychoanalytic Institute, Miami; Adjunct Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, University of Miami School of Medicine.

It is unusual these days to encounter a psychoanalytic book that speaks both to professionals and to a broader audience; when done well, as this book is, such a presentation is doubly valuable. Although psychoanalytic ideas and findings are meaningful to everyone, and are not the exclusive property and concern of insiders, few analytic works (apart from critiques and histories of the field) have been designed of late for the general educated reader, as was commonly done in earlier generations. I suspect that this shift toward greater insularity is in part due to the pressure of psychoanalytic group attitudes, and that it is therefore relevant to the subject matter of Ethel Person's nuanced, yet very accessible study of the psychology of power and powerlessness. Ever ready to equate popularization with dilution, innovation with deviation, and simplicity with simplification, the culture of psychoanalysis has not been very welcoming or encouraging to writing of this kind, and that is a shame.

As Person tells us in Feeling Strong, and shows by her own example in writing it, “feeling strong” and having a sense of “authentic power” can be precious outcomes of insightfully attained trust in, and appropriate expression of, one's distinctive, even idiosyncratic feelings, strivings, and opinions. A corollary is that conservatism and conformity are not only contrary to the spirit of inquiry so vital to a science; they can also disempower a discipline's practitioners.

To the wider audience, Feeling Strong offers information and wisdom about psychological development, typical conflicts and their solutions, human nature and its inherent difficulties, and the value to be gained from profound self-examination that leads to understanding and acceptance of one's human qualities in the context of a particular endowment and history.

267

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Elephant in the Consulting Room

Managed Care and the Practice of Psychotherapy

Julie Cohen , Jane Gillham and Jeanne Marecek

Jeanne Marecek

21 River Road
Frenchtown, NJ 08825
E-mail: jlcohen98@hotmail.com

Jeanne Marecek  

Therapists are constrained in many ways by managed care—by limits on the number of sessions, by restrictions on the problems that qualify for reimbursement, and by pressures to use inexpensive and brief forms of treatment. We ask how such constraints alter processes at the heart of therapy—how therapy begins and ends, how the therapist-client relationship develops, and how clients and therapists conceive of their work together. We addressed these issues through intensive interviews with sixteen therapists from diverse theoretical backgrounds. The interviews were analyzed using an interpretive approach derived from grounded theory (Glaser and Straus 1967).

Results

For every therapist interviewed, some aspects of managed care were in conflict with their ethos of good care. This fundamental conflict was the root of deep discontent. Some reported that managed care restricted therapy to superficial goals and quick-fix solutions. Some reported that managed care policies disallow the types of therapy they deem most effective (e.g., psychodynamic therapies). Some could no longer continue various practices that promote and support client change, such as school consultations, family meetings, home visits, and formal clinical assessments. Such exclusions were based on financial considerations, not scientific evidence of efficacy.

Therapists also voiced a variety of ethical concerns. As they saw it, they often had to choose between honest reports about their clients and good care. To provide adequate care, they felt it necessary to conceal certain elements of their work, misrepresent diagnoses, and distort reports of clients' progress. Some therapists drew their clients into misrepresenting diagnoses and into other deceptions, a practice that may ultimately erode public confidence in the profession. Others found themselves bound by “gag” rules that prevent them from providing clients accurate information about their problems and their treatment.

Some therapists reported that clients who had entered therapy through the managed care system had received distorted ideas about therapy.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Practice in the Early Postgraduate Years

Sabrina Cherry , Deborah Cabaniss , Dana Haywood , Nicholas Forand and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

585 West End Avenue
Suite 1E
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: sc26@columbia.edu

Steven Roose  

Educators generally believe that developing proficiency in psychoanalysis requires significant clinical immersion and, thus, that considerable professional development occurs in the years after graduation from analytic training. While we know that graduate analysts continue their control cases, there are no empirical data on whether or how graduates further develop their analytic skills after training (Cherry et al. 2004; Glick et al. 1996). Neither the period from graduation through training analyst appointment nor the career path for analysts who do not seek training analyst appointment has ever been systematically studied.

To explore postgraduate professional development, we have initiated a longitudinal study of analytic careers. As a prelude to this larger effort, we conducted this survey of the last fifteen years of graduates from the Columbia Psychoanalytic Center for Training and Research. The survey asked graduates to describe both their analytic practice and their interest in pursuing training and supervising analyst appointment.

The 23-item questionnaire was completed by 66% of respondents (67/102). Graduates practiced a mean of 30 hours per week, and 39% had a salaried job in addition to private practice. The study identified two subgroups of graduates. The first subgroup are the noncertified, nontraining analysts (GAs), 78% of respondents (52/67). The second subgroup are the certified and training analysts (CAs and TAs), 22% of respondents (15/67). The GAs began a mean of 1.4 new cases since graduation, as compared to CAs and TAs, who began a mean of 5.4 and 8.3 new cases, respectively. CAs and TAs practiced more analysis and saw more twice-weekly therapy cases than GAs did. The most commonly practiced treatment for all subgroups was at frequencies of once a week or less. This finding is consistent with membership studies done by the American Psychoanalytic Association (Brauer and Brauer 1998).

[[RunningHead]]

Resistance

How Do We Think Of It In The Twenty-First Century?

Eslee Samberg

Eslee Samberg

2211 Broadway
Office IH
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-874-2349
E-mail: esamberg@aol.com

Eslee Samberg   Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 24, 2003.

Arnold Cooper, chairing the panel, placed the concept of resistance within the current pluralistic landscape of psychoanalysis. He noted that while one segment of the psychoanalytic community views resistance analysis as crucial, other segments (e.g., the object relational, the intersubjectivist, and the interpersonal) may view resistance as an outdated concept connoting an adversarial and authoritative stance. Using a graph captioned “Trends in Usage of the Term Resistance,” Cooper noted the increasing occurrence of references to resistance in two major psychoanalytic journals since the 1980s (JAPA and the International Journal of Psychoanalysis). Whatever the challenge to its conceptual utility, the term is in one sense very much alive.

Cooper posed a series of questions that served to position resistance in relation to issues of contemporary interest: transference and intrapsychic conflict; the narcissistic threat of interpretation; the sense of intimate struggle within the treatment relationship; and anxiety about change, loss, the unknown, emptiness, etc. He concluded by asking whether a concept that raises so many questions can still serve a useful purpose.

Eslee Samberg, the panel's reporter, presented a brief literature review from an historical perspective. She emphasized that over time the understanding of resistance has reflected each writer's conceptualization of what psychoanalysis is and how it works.

Panelists: Arnold Cooper (chair), Robert Michels, Anna Ornstein, Daniel Stern, David Tuckett.

[[RunningHead]]

Premature Termination From Psychoanalysis

An Investigation of Factors Contributing to Early Endings

Edith Cooper , Margaret Hamilton , Dinu Gangure and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

440 West End Avenue, #1D
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: emc18@columbia.edu

Steven Roose  

Within the setting of a psychoanalytic training institute, the early ending of a control case may have important educational ramifications, including fewer opportunities to deepen the analytic process and a slower progression through training. A better understanding of the factors that contribute to early termination might be useful both in case selection for psychoanalysis and in maintaining the treatment once begun, thus enhancing candidates' educational experience.

The present sample included all control cases who initiated treatment with a candidate analyst at the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research from 1995 to 2000. Patients entered analysis either by applying to the center's clinic specifically for psychoanalysis, or by conversion from the private psychotherapy practices of candidates. Data were obtained by a review of the charts kept at the center. All patients signed a consent form for treatment and research. For the purposes of this study, the cases designated as “premature terminations” were ones in which the candidate analyst made a chart note indicating an abrupt or early termination.

Over a six-year span, 163 patients were accepted to begin psycho-analysis as control cases at the center.
