[[RunningHead]]

Treatment

Infecting The Treatment: Being an Hiv-Positive Analyst. By Gilbert Cole. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2002, 210 pp., $39.95.

Henry Friedman

Henry Friedman

6 Garden Terrace
Cambridge, MA 02138
Fax: 617-876-1237
E-mail: friedmanhj@aol.com

Henry Friedman   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Teaching and Supervising Analyst, Massachusetts Institute of Psychoanalysis.

At first glance, the title of this book might indicate that it will be of use primarily to analysts in one of two special interest categories: first, those who deal with an HIV-positive, homosexual group of patients and, second, those interested in how psychoanalysts deal with an illness of their own while working actively with patients. As a reviewer, I am concerned that such an interpretation of the title will discourage readers with a more general interest in psychoanalysis and, in so doing, prevent them from encountering this unusual, stimulating book, which makes a significant contribution to our understanding of the nature of the psychoanalytic relationship and the specific importance of the analyst's considered self-disclosure as part of that relationship. Gilbert Cole has used his experience of being infected with HIV to write about the psychoanalytic encounter with a depth and honesty that is both refreshing and original. There is much more to be found here than the act of bravery involved in the author's coming out to his colleagues regarding his positive HIV status; as much as he writes from the heart, he does so with a distinct gift for putting his experience in an intellectual frame-work that will enhance most psychoanalysts' appreciation of the complexity of the analytic relationship and the potential effectiveness of self-disclosure. While clearly relational in his approach, Cole is thoroughly familiar with the traditional ego psychological approach and its justification for restricting the analyst's activity through the technical requirements of neutrality, anonymity, and abstinence. This enables him not only to illustrate his greater freedom of participation in his analytic work but also to explain why he believes that the restrictions on the analyst's activity in the classical model are of questionable necessity.

953

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Structural and symptomatic change in psychoanalysis and psychodynamic psychotherapy of young adults

A quantitative study of process and outcome

Andrew Gerber , Peter Fonagy , Anthony Bateman and Anna Higgitt

Anna Higgitt

525 East 68th Street, Box 140
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: agerber@aya.yale.edu

Anna Higgitt  

This quasi-experimental study explores the interrelationship of measures of structural change, symptomatic change, and psychotherapeutic process in a sample of 25 young adults sequentially assigned to psychoanalysis (n = 14) or psychodynamic psychotherapy (n = 11) at the Anna Freud Centre in London. The Young Adult Weekly Rating Scale (YAWRS), an 899-item questionnaire, was used by analysts to record the presence of manifest, unconscious, transferential, and procedural themes in psychotherapy and psychoanalytic sessions and whether these themes were interpreted by the analyst. Patients were assessed by an independent psychiatrist at intake, at termination, and at 18-month intervals after intake and termination with Main and Goldwyn's Adult Attachment Interview (AAI) and on a host of symptomatic and diagnostic measures (SADS-L for DSM Axis I, SCID-II for DSM Axis II, BDI for depression, STAI for anxiety, SAS-M for social adjustment, and SCL-90 for general psychiatric symptomatology).

The patients in this study were 18 to 25 years old, depressed, anxious, and personality disordered. Most had undergone previous psycho-therapeutic treatment, and several had been on medications (though none were currently). Over the course of the treatments, ranging from one to eight years in length, 12 of 19 patients for whom adequate data were collected improved symptomatically according to an aggregate measure of symptoms and diagnoses. Ten of the 12 improvers were in the psychoanalysis treatment group, suggesting that it is a more effective treatment in this population. Data from 1,314 YAWRS questionnaires were factor analyzed and used to operationalize a set of hypotheses derived from the psychotherapy process literature.

In the first year of psychoanalytic treatments (as compared with psychodynamic psychotherapy), higher scores were found on therapist dynamic technique (particularly general interpretation and transference interpretation), patient dynamic material (particularly transference themes), and negative patient transference.

[[RunningHead]]

The Multimedia Database for Developmental Studies at the Pacella Parent Child Center'S Observational Nursery

Ezequiel Galarce , Cheryl Williams , Andres Roussos and Wilma Bucci

Wilma Bucci

3400 North Charles Street
Baltimore, MD 21211
E-mail: galarce@jhu.edu

Wilma Bucci  

The Pacella Parent Child Center (http://www.theparentchildcenter.org) of the New York Psychoanalytic Society is developing a unique archive containing a wide range of data on parent-child interaction that is available to qualified researchers. The data include audiotapes and videotapes of parents, children, and staff, interviews with mothers and fathers, and coded transcriptions of the audio material and questionnaires. The archive reflects the psychoanalytic focus of the investigators who designed it, but the data are of general interest for a wide range of investigations. The data are being organized in a centralized database, with emphasis on security and effective retrieval capabilities in response to particular questions.

While over the past several decades many important studies have been carried out based on observations of mother-child interactions, to the best of our knowledge none has compiled a systematic archive including raw data together with findings potentially available for external use, and none has included the types of observations we include in our database.

1246

[[RunningHead]]

KEY CONCEPTS IN PSYCHOANALYSIS. By Stephen Frosh. New York: New York University Press, 2003, 112 pp., $60.00 hardcover, $18.00 paperback.

Ronald Baker

Ronald Baker

171 Wellesley Avenue South
Norwich NR1 4AD Norfolk
UNITED KINGDOM
E-mail: ronaldbaker@clara.co.uk

Ronald Baker   Training and Supervising Analyst, British Psychoanalytical Society; member, Royal College of Psychiatrists.

In this slim volume Stephen Frosh tackles the daunting task of making core psychoanalytic concepts available in a comprehensible way to lay public and psychoanalytic scholars alike. His starting point is that

1290

despite the current criticism leveled at psychoanalysis, Freud's basic ideas have continued to prove appealing to students, the general population, and, not least, professionals and their patients struggling to make sense of themselves and their intense emotional conflicts.

He rightly maintains that Freudian thinking has entered into our culture in areas such as cinema, art, and literary criticism. Moreover, it has profoundly shaped our understanding of the problems around forming and maintaining deep and satisfying interpersonal relationships.

Frosh describes and defines each concept, thereby providing an overview of the therapeutic and cultural uses of central psychoanalytic terms. Examples of these concepts include the unconscious, repression, projection, fantasy, the oedipus complex, interpretation, resistance, interpretation, transference, and countertransference. It is apparent and no surprise that disputes relating to the key concepts of psychoanalysis are inevitable and will not easily be resolved. The text, therefore, is spirited and often controversial, not least because psychoanalytic concepts are complex and subject to manifold definitions and understandings. For this reason, practicing psychoanalysts will return to refresh and expand their understanding of key concepts throughout their careers.

Given the intricacy of the field and the nature and roots of the controversies, it seems unlikely that the promise that these concepts will be made truly available to a nonexpert readership and beginning students can be fulfilled. Indeed, the experienced psychoanalyst might well be seriously stretched in grappling with the difficult and intriguing arguments that arise throughout the volume. This, of course, is no bad thing; it appears to me the very important challenge posed by Frosh's approach.
